No problem. Throw one or two of these gems into your paper, and you'll sound like you just came back from an extended vacation with Sartre, Heidegger, Gadamer, and other completely mind-bending philosophers.

title: ResGenie 2.2.1
description: ResGenie 2.2 is my first major revision to the original ResGenie of last year. I now support the addcolor external, full easy FX, multiple FX on opencard, new spreadhseet style windows for viewing resources, a ppat tool for easy use of ppat's with addcolor, new and improved control palettes, a new dialog for importing PICT files (allows previews-and corrects a problem where i forgot to write a renumber resource routine last time around-ouch), creates color ready stacks with superior scripts that don't blitz regular fx throughout hypercard, doesn't mess any of your menus up, doesn't take you into a 'color mode' that stops everything else. And more, much more, such as a 'link' tool that creates dynamic links between color cards and stacks--writing the correct scripts and giving you a pop up menu of color fx to choose from; a color object report tool that gives you access to all of addcolor's database info at the click of a mouse ( card or bg id's, and object ids in the case of buttons and fields). ResGenie objects no longer contain the full scripts for changing effects, etc., but still *do provide useful info* in their script headers. Includes handy 'Object' tools for listing, aligning, editing objects (a modeless real Mac window). An 'Objects' window that gives you a list of all objects, card and bg levels, lets you rename, edit contents, nudge point by point, change the visible, edit the script--all without putting you in a modal dialog (of course not). Locate hidden fields and buttons, or those dreaded '0,0,0,0' coordinate objects that you didn't even realize were there. Grab the text of locked fields without having to putter with the field tool. Get info on externals in your stacks, read the hex or ascii output from addcolor database, preview or view pictures, play sounds, record sounds (in your stack or any other file)--with more compression available than through audio palette, and more... ResGenie installs *nothing* into your home stack, doesn't work unless you tell it to, and doesn't care where it is--you push the activate button and it's ready. ResGenie adds *no* background noise to the message hierarchy (i.e., doesn't have an on openstack handler that runs every time you open any stack)... Color authoring has never been so easy or painless. Resource importing has never been so modeless. Try it. I think you'll like what you see. A small 'getting started' stack has been included to help you see the full potential of ResGenie. Import PICT files, Import resources, record sounds (any file), play sounds, get info on externals, delete resources, move resources between files, modeless operation. Create custom icons (thumbnails) for PICT and MooV files, create FX tools for addcolor, a linking tool for addcolor navigation buttons with color FX (similar to the old 'link' tool, but writes scripts for color effects as you link). Report tool for getting full addcolor database info on any color object. Much, much more. *ResGenie 2.2.1 contains some routines for helping out in low memory situations. I also included some notes on recommended RAM settings for HyperCard. Still $10. MC ------------------------ The Macintosh HyperCard Forum thanks this uploader for including Keywords at the top of the file description. For more info on uploading files and for tips on increasing the number of folks who download your files - see the document '*** Uploaders please read this' at the top of the New Files library.

title: TOXIX 2.01 Demo
description: TOXIX provides information on toxic properties for nearly a thousand chemical substances. Each compound is characterized by name, structural formula (i.e. chemical structure), CAS (Chemical Abstracts Service) registry number, and by the type of functional groups present. Toxic properties and OSHA air exposure limits - when established - are given for each chemical substance. Hazardous properties were obtained from catalogs of chemical suppliers and from product labels and were placed in eleven different categories : known carcinogen, suspect carcinogen, teratogen, mutagen, highly toxic, toxic, irritant, lachrymator, corrosive, oxidizer, no significant hazardous properties. Exposure limits for air contaminants -current as of October 1993 - are based on the OSHA Safety and Health Standards [1900.1000 : 7-1-91 Edition].

title: PenPat/Mode/Size Mix & Match
description: The XFCNs in this stack demonstrate some QuickDraw techniques using CompileIt! There are a bunch of radio buttons that allow you to select the penMode and penPat to be used in the demonstration. Mix and match until you get the desired effect. The XFCNs simply draw a vertical or horizontal line of the length specified with the width, mode and pattern chosen. The line is dragged horizontally or vertically tracking to the moues. The demonstration is set up in such a manner as to emulate split bars for hyperCard fields. I specifically set up this stack to allow me to try differenct quick draw combinations on different Macs as I was experiencing results different on PowerBooks than on CRT based Macs. Gary Stanoulis CompileIt! SIG Leader

title: PrefsCleaner 1.0.1
description: PrefsCleaner is a HyperCard stack which helps you to manage your preferences folder. In fact, our Preferences folders are often full of useless preferences files, often created by demo programs or applications used a time only or deleted. Each of these creates his own preferences file in the Preferences folder. At end, our Prefs Folder will be full of useless prefs files, which only occupy disk space and make the MacOS and other applications unstable. PrefsCleaner scans your preferences folder for files which are probably useless, then you can decide which of these you want to delete.

The stack allows you to keep track of each new lead, note the job type and the source, record actions taken, sort leads by type, source, or by date of last action, and print a simple report in one of two formats. You can mark/unmark cards, find any word, and create new cards at the push of a button. The cards are automatically dated and numbered, and a help screen is included. It's proven very useful in my own job hunting. This stack is shareware: if you find a job with the help of this stack, please find ME a job or send $10! Good luck!

title: LaunchFile XCMD
description: This bug-fix release replaces the previous version dated July 19th, which had a memory leak that could cause problems if you called LaunchFile repeatedly (perhaps not until you called it hundred of times, though...). Nothing else has changed. LaunchFile is a HyperCard XCMD I wrote to provide 'more convenient' launching under System 7. I wrote it to use with PowerKey, but it will work with any program that uses HyperCard XCMDs. Give LaunchFile a pathname (full or partial) to a file, and it will do what's necessary to open the file. It can handle applications, documents, desk accessories, and aliases to all of these. The nice thing about the way documents are handled is that LaunchFile will look up the creating application; no need (or way) for you to specify it. This is supplied in a HyperCard 2.x stack - use ResEdit to extract the XCMD. Stuffit 1.5.1 archive.

title: MorphileX XFCN 1.0
description: MorphileX XFCN 1.0 Sunday, June 21, 1998 Software Perspectives - MorphileX XFCN -------------------------------------------------- MorphileX is a powerful new XFCN from the makers of PlayMOD and SpeakText. It enables you to either get or set the creator and type of a file from within HyperCard or SuperCard. The target file is also updated instantly, no waiting for the Finder to catch up. MorphileX utilizes our recently developed Command Processor technology to make it easier to issue commands to the XFCN. This archive contains a stack which includes complete and detailed information on syntax and commands, which can be printed for easier reading or reference. Use in public domain, shareware or commercial software requires a distribution license. Please see the included Licensing Information document for details. Copyright (c)1994 Software Perspectives. title: Hanrek XCMD Shell 1.2
description: This XCMD shell is unique, in that it makes it possible for one to be immediately successful creating XCMDs in C without having to know anything about the inner workings and mechanisms of HyperCard externals. If you have, say, a function in C that you would like to call as an XCMD or XFCN, all you have to do is to open a single source file, paste the function in, copy and paste in a couple of statements and adjust them slightly (to hook up with any parameters and return result), compile it, and you're outta there! If you do not have version 7 of Symantec C, (Think C) then no problem. A HyperCard stack shows you visually the settings you need to make for a Think C project of any version. Things are structured so that you can get in and get out quickly, yet you also can get in to everything if you want. In addition, there are extensive notes to help with the commonly confusing things, such as working with handles, pointers, strings, etc.

title: Learning Processes
description: Some psychologists argue that we learn by trial and error, gradually coming to associate stimulus and response with one another. Other psychologists argue that we learn by insight, meaning that solutions to problems most often occur spontaneously and without conscious deliberation. How much truth is there to these opposing positions? One way to find out is to study your behavior in this problem solving task. This stack has been checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6

title: Lotto Calc
description: Well here it is folks, a sure fire bet to win every 49 Lotto around. No, just kidding. But hey, this at least makes picking the numbers fun. This stack puts your Mac to work generating up to 24 randomly generated, 6 number Lotto combinations. It also lets you print them out if you are so bold as to believe in them. To make it more interesting there are some pretty interesting sounds you can have played as each number is chosen. A wise sage told me that you can tell which lotto combination is the winning one by carefully evaluating the sounds played during its generation. There is no rule as to what the sounds mean, it is going to be a purely gut feel. For those of you that are budding HyperCard scriptors the scripts in this stack are fully commented for your inspection and nothing is locked. This is freeware. I only have one requirement. If you win with one of the number combinations generated by this stack, you have to pay me half of your winnings. Oh well, it was worth a try, right;) Good luck and have fun!

title: Vocabulary Situations Lite
description: 'Vocabulary Situations Lite' Phil Shapiro posted a text file in this area in February requesting that someone build a stack around it. He described the file as follows: 'This short text file is a series of 'vocabulary situations' ...' '... Each vocabulary situation describes a scenario where students are called upon to choose from one of two options. To choose the correct option, though, students needs to understand the vocabulary used in describing the scenario.' This stack is my response to his request. The stack contains the thirty situations in the list, has room for up to 170 more, and the stack can be adapted for other flash card purposes. See the Help stack for instructions on how to edit the data. A version containing more sound resources was uploaded to AOL earlier. Since the size of the stack with all the extra data might have been off-putting to potential downloaders (there have been only 17 downloads to date - we should have named it Naked Girl Situations), I split the stack and put the sounds and some utilities in a stack called 'VS Sounds'. You might call this version 'Vocabulary Situations Lite'. The functionality of the stack is the same, except that it looks for the stack 'VS Sounds' and starts using the sounds in that stack for transitions. If it doesn't find it the first time, it never looks again. You don't need 'VS Sounds' unless you want a larger variety of sounds or you want to easily record or scrounge or manipulate the sounds to your own taste. As always, response is requested. Fillmore 051695

title: Stack Dissector 1.1.1
description: Ooops! Never change anything trivial at the last minute. Things don't work when you do that! The help should work better in this version. Sorry for wasting people's time and money. I see over 100 people downloaded this stack in the last week or so. Wow! That's great. Thank you for showing interest. How come only one person sent me mail to tell me something was wrong with my stack? If it wasn't for Kevin I never would have known. If you don't tell me what's wrong it'll never get fixed. Please take the time to let me know what you think. Anyways back to the advertising ... Ever want to know how a stack is put together? Ever lose a button or field or handler? Ever want to tweak a stack remotely? This is the stack for you. Stack Dissector 1.1.1 is a stack browser that allows you to look through the component parts of a stack by clicking on the names of objects that interest you. Objects can be stacks, backgrounds, cards, fields, buttons, scripts or handlers. When you click on an object it expands to show the objects it contains. Shift click and you go to that object in the target stack. Option click and the dialog box for the object appears. Shareware, $10. If you like it send some mail to MSSeward. If you don't like it send some mail to Grimbeorn. Any feedback is greatly appreciated. Downloads of previous version: 138

title: PowerList 2.0
description: ----------------- First off, let me apologize for the size of this stack. As you will see, I tried extra hard to explain every possible detail about this new version so I ended up with quite a large stack. In addition to that, I also included a PowerList installer XCMD to assist you in moving PowerList among your various stacks. PowerList is a XFCN that brings up a dialog box containing a scrollable list of information stored in a container. PowerList includes several features missing in other similar externals. PowerList has the ability to accept input by line (return-delimited), by item (comma-delimited), by STR or STR# resource, or by any text file. PowerList will optionally sort the list before displaying it in the dialog. PowerList accepts several different 'parameters' which determine varying selection criteria of the list. It will also accept a prompt string for display in its dialog box. PowerList allows you change the font of the dialog box to any currently available font, and the dialog itself can be placed anywhere on the screen. PowerList 2.0 introduces a new and exciting trend in how the user determines all of the available features operate and how the dialog box looks on the screen. The user now supplies a 'configuration string' to PowerList that contains several 'keywords' that determine the exact function of each individual feature. And the greatest of all features, Cloning liscenses!

Anyway I decided to take him up on his suggestion! As he put it, 'Anyway if you are going to be insulted by your computer, I think it might as well be me doing the insulting.' Sounds include: Guess again!, I don't think so!, Is this FUN for you?, Read the documentation, Switch colas, That's wrong!, What a blunder!, What a clown!. This stack is free to anyone EXCEPT companies that are in the business of collecting and selling Public Domain/Shareware stuff. title: Kaleidagraph AppleEvent Scrip...
description: Kaleidagraph AppleEvent Scrip... Sunday, June 21, 1998 The file is a self extracting archive containing two stacks, one for scripting and one to provide online scripting help, and a documentation file in MS Word format. The scripting stack contains the SendAppleEvent XCMD written by Donald Olsen of Apple Computer, which enable HC 2.1 to send AppleEvents of arbitrary class and ID to any AppleEvent-aware program. The stacks have been set up to work with and provide online help for the scripting of Kaleidagraph by Synergy Software, which in version 3.0 adds AppleEvent support to a fine program for interactive data visualization and analysis. The stacks can be used with Kaleidagraph 3.0 without any detailed knowledge of AppleEvents, but a knowledge of Kaleidagraph internal scripting is needed, since AppleEvents are used simply to send text scripts to Kaleidagraph. The help stack documents all scripting features of KG3, and buttons are provided to access the Kaleidagraph general help utility. These stacks were derived from similar stacks which were distributed with Spyglass Transform 2.1. It should be fairly straightforward to adapt them to any AppleEvent-aware program to accomplish bulk file processing, given documentation of the AppleEvents understood by the target program and a working knowledge of HyperCard scripting.

title: Mac Safe 2.0
description: Mac Safe 2.0 is a safety utillity that helps keep those nosey prying eyes of your desktop and out of your Hard Drive. This is a full version. No functions are disabled. You will need a 14' color monitor that displays 256 or more colors to run this software. People or persons trying to access your Hard Drive or any part of your computer system while this program is running will be totally unsuccessful in their act. Mac Safe produces a false desktop when used on a 14' 256 color monitor. This desktop illusion contains a security field that when touched in the wrong place will set off a security alert box. This alert will produce a dialog that tells you what time the Mac was illegally accessed. Send comments to Roblow.

Version 1.2 includes three new features. Each PowerPalette enhances specific HyperCard functions. For example, the Properties palette accelerates screen design and working with fields and buttons. Its moving, alignment and spacing tools bring MacDraw type functionality to HyperCard. Interactively change any property of grouped fields and/or buttons without wading through HyperCard dialog boxes. The Pictures palette makes it easy for anyone to use PICT resources within their stacks. Open PICT images from any available stack, and modify their style, magnification, rect, and location. Move a PICT window around the screen with a standard click-and-drag motion. Click the Script button to automatically generate all the script commands required to redisplay it. Other PowerPalettes include Messages, Sorter, Replacer and Caser. All provide valuable new capabilities for advanced or casual HyperCard users. To unlock your copy of PowerPalettes call 1-800-668-3873 (TOLL-FREE!). Provide the Registration Key for your Macintosh (it's in the PowerPalettes stack) and a VISA or MasterCard number. A License Key will be faxed or phoned to you within 24 hours. Your cost is just $89 US ($99 Cdn. plus applicable taxes) If you prefer to have a free copy of PowerPalettes mailed to you EMail your address to CHESHIR. It's FREE!

title: HyperAlgebra
description: HyperAlgebra - A HyperCard Stack which generates random tests from a databank of problems on adding/subtracting, multiplying, and factoring algebraic expressions. It uses a HyperCard XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein that uses MathLinkÂ¨ to communicate with MathematicaÂ¨. Stacks by Carol and Donald Hazlewood MathLinkÂ¨ XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein Inspired by HyperMathLink, a HyperCard Stack written by Doug Stein and John Bonadies. Â©1993 Redwood Software Permission granted to distribute these files freely provided this notice and all comments stay intact. Version 1.0 (28 May 1993) --------------------------- HYPERALGEBRA TEST GENERATOR HyperAlgebra, by clicking on a button, generates an algebra test of 10 problems in the appropriate area by randomly selecting problems from a databank of problems, allows the user to enter an answer to the problem, and checks to see if the entered answer is correct. If not, a hint is offered as to what might be incorrect and requests the user to try again. If the correct answer is not entered in 3 tries, an offer is made to give the correct solution and the user can then go to the next problem. On completion, a score is tabulated, with 10 points awarded if a problem is corrected answered on the first try, x points if answered on the second try, y points if answered on the third try, and 0 points if missed three times. This score with the users name, the time elapsed, and the particular problems selected randomly from the databank is recorded in a stack in the Resources folder, "Scores." HyperAlgebra uses MathematicaÂ¨ via the MathLink XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein for the appropriate algebraic manipulations. This means that when you start a HyperAlgebra test, MathematicaÂ¨ is automatically launched. GETTING STARTED The HyperAlgebra window has three buttons, currently, for generating tests on adding/subtracting, multiplying, and factoring algebraic expressions. The user can enter a solution by clicking buttons or directly typing in entry field of the test stack. A Mathematica Kernel is launched initially by using either the "Link to MathematicaÂ¨" button or the "Check" button. TECHNICAL NOTES HyperAlgebra uses an XFCN by Chris Rogers and Doug Stein that in turn uses the MathLinkÂ¨ protocol to talk to a Mathematica Kernel. By editing the script for the "Link to MathematicaÂ¨" button, you can make this stack communicate with a Mathematica Kernel running on another Macintosh (or even another brand of computer)! The folder "Local" contains a MathematicaÂ¨ package "1311.m" that defines two functions "CompareSolution" and "CompareFactors" when a Mathematica Kernel is activated. PACKING LIST About HyperAlgebra - this document HyperAlgebra - a HyperCard Stack Local - a Macintosh folder containing: 1311.m - a MathematicaÂ¨ package defining the functions "CompareSolution" and "CompareFactors" used by the .test Stacks Resources - a Macintosh folder containing: Add_Subtract.test - a HyperCard Stack generating the add/subtract test Factor.test - a HyperCard Stack generating the factor test Multiply.test - a HyperCard Stack generating the multiply test Scores - a HyperCard Stack which collects scores on the various tests ExpBuilder - a HyperCard Stack used by the .test Stacks Frac2Builder - a HyperCard Stack used by the .test Stacks VarChoice - a HyperCard Stack used by the .test Stacks Data - a Macintosh folder containing: Add_Subtract.data - a MicroSoft WordÂ¨ document with test data Factor.data - a MicroSoft WordÂ¨ document with test data Multiply.data - a MicroSoft WordÂ¨ document with test data

title: Mental Giant
description: Mental Giant is a HyperCard stack which develops one's ability to perform mental math. Click the 'Begin' button to begin (what else?). After determining your choice of either 'Silent' or 'Speaking' version, your gender, and asking your name, Mental Giant will then ask a series of twenty-five mental math problems. Type in the answer when presented with a dialog box and either push return, enter, or click OK. Players are timed how long it takes them to finish. The smaller the amount of time it takes a player to finish, the higher his or her standing in the 'Masters' listing; only the lowest ten are listed. (Hint: the silent version runs a little faster). You may check the listings before you begin by clicking the 'Scores' button. These listings will also be shown at the end should your score be listed. A 'thermometer' (Mental Measure) along the right hand side of the card registers your progress. The rest is practice, and a little 'evolution.' If you enjoyed my stack, 'The House of Multiplication Horror,' you will enjoy this stack as well.

Progress XCMD 2.0 provides a much more elegant alternative for providing visual feed-back that everything is proceeding normally. It displays a choice of 2 dialogs containing graphic displays. One is a scale with a 'diamond' symbol that moves along to indicate 'Percent Complete'. The dialog box contains your prompt string as to what is going on and can be positioned anywhere on the screen (it is centered on the card window by default). title: Area Codes/Postal Abbreviatio...

I use them to standardize the entries in a 'state' field of data stack with the official 2 letter postal code. Using this I then verify the telephone number in the 'phone' field by checking the area code versus the state code. 1) cbAreaCode() --- pass any telephone area code to it and it will return that 2 letter abbreviation for the appropriate state, territory or canadian province. Pass any 2 letter abbreviation for the above and it will return all the appropriate area codes. 2) cbPostAbbrev() - pass to it the full name of any state/territory or canadian prov. and it all return the official 2 letter postal abbreviation. title: HTML Optimizer 3.0.3
description: HTML Optimizer offers FOUR functions: 1. HTML Optimizer saves 10 to 15% of space on your web pages by removing unnecessary characters and tags. Though this may seem a small improvement, load time savings is much higher as your client's Internet browser parses the pages more efficient. Embedded script code is not ruined by HTML Optimizer! Your original pages remain untouched for later updating. New text files are created with same Creator as original and are put in a folder named "Optimizer Output". Take care that you do not replace your original HTML pages with optimized ones because you may lose your nicely indented layout. Keep the originals in a safe place. 2. Another useful function of HTML Optimizer is checking the socalled tagged pairs, i.e. the tags that always have to appear together with their end tags, for example .... All dangling tags are reported. Further, the IMG tags are checked for the presence of the Width, Height and Alt attributes. The Width and Height attributes help the browser to build up the page faster because it can reserve the necessary room for the image while placing the text. The Alt attributes make your pages more user-friendly. 3. The Mark function does the same as the Check function, but produces new files with the incomplete tags marked with the character •, so that you can easily find them for fixing. 4. The Convert function takes care that special characters between ASCII 128 and 255 are converted to the appropriate &.....; notation.

title: Hyper-Cooking 1.1.1

This is accomplished by using the Generic Import feature built into Hermes, along with a little HyperCard Scriptery! The file name becomes the message title, and its creation date becomes the message creation date. Message sender and receiver are configurable. A help section on the card offers more specific details & instructions for use. Files are limited to 15k, by Hermes. Any questions or comments? Contact me at The Association Mac BBS, 313-695-6744 (14.4k HST). Enjoy!

title: HCSC 052295 Text-to-Speech
description: The enclosed stacks and documents were part of a package that was issued in connection with the 5/22/95 America Online HyperCard Forum Scripting Conference. The HyperCard Scripting Conference is simply an ongoing series of online meetings in the AOL HyperCard conference area (Shortcut: MHC), usually held on Monday evenings. Some of these meetings have formal topics with guest presenters, others are informal get togethers where HyperCard scripting enthusiasts can ask and answer questions on any topic as well as share scripts and tips. Anyone with an AOL account can attend. The subject of the 5/22/95 conference was HyperCard and the text-to-speech capabilities provided by the Speech Manager Extension. Although America Online provides the venue for the conferences, it is in no way responsible for them and does not, in any way, own or endorse the contents of the discussions or the associated stacks. Since the conference there have been a number of changes to the Speech Manager. As of this writing, we are up to version 1.4.1. I have not had a chance to do a comprehensive check of the behavior of the latest versions, so I can't guarantee that the notes about bugs and quirks remain accurate. However, the scripting suggestions and the guide to the use of inline commands and the phonemic symbols should remain generally useful. There are some important differences between the versions of the Speech Manager that were available when the stack was created and the latest version. First of all, it is now possible to set the default voice using the Speech control panel. When the stack was created, the default was restricted to the voice "Fred". The examples in the stack will probably work better if you set the default to "Fred". Since there is no longer a built in default voice or a built in synthesizer, you must, contrary to the information in the stack, have at least one voice installed in the Voices folder in the Extensions folder as well as a synthesizer for text-to-speech to work. Another important difference is that there are now three Speech synthesizers available, Macintalk II, Macintalk III, and Macintalk Pro. Few HyperCard scripters realize that is is possible to embed special commands into text that is to be spoken. These commands allow one to have much greater control over how the synthesizer "speaks" the text. Perhaps the greatest value of this stack is that it documents this feature. Before closing this note, I would especially like to thank to Jeff Crossley, who organized the HyperCard Scripting Conference, and who authored the template for the Script Conference demo stacks. Mark Klink

title: Version Control 2.1.SIT
description: Two external commands are attached to this stack. They give the stack developer a way to attach version numbers and comments to a stack in a 'vers' resource (like the resource that is attached to the HyperCard application). A Macintosh 'vers' resource stores 4 items: the version number in BCD form, the version number in ascii (text), a version comment, and a number indicating the country the file is localized for. If you "Get Info" on a file in Finder 6.1 or later, the version comment is the text that is displayed. The other data is available to any Mac program that opens the resource. You can use the ReadVersion XFCN to read the version number, the version comment, and the country code. It returns them as comma-separated items in a text string. (The comment will be enclosed in quotes.) You need to be in the stack in order to read its 'vers' data, so go there first if you're not. ReadVersion will return an error message if anything goes wrong. To change your stack's 'vers' resource, use the WriteVersion XCMD. It takes 2 or 3 parameters: the version number, the version comment and, optionally, the country code. It will update the 'vers' resource of the current stack, (or create one if none existed). Check 'the result' for error messages after calling WriteVersion.

title: Pediatric Diagnosis #2(PEDDX2...
description: These Hypercard stacks require Hypercard v. 2 and because of their size must be run from either a hard disk or a 1.4 MB floppy drive. The hard disk is the preferred storage medium since they run v-e-r-y s-l-o-w-l-y on a floppy drive. This is the second of a series of interactive Hypercard stacks designed to test one's ability to approach the diagnosis of a pediatric patient's illness in a logical and thoughtful fashion. The stacks are designed so as to be as close to 'real life' as is possible, including the element of time. You are given the history of three patients' illnesses and pertinent physical findings. After that you may request those laboratory studies you deem necessary until you feel certain of the diagnosis and proper therapy for each patient. The 'grades' you receive are based upon the cost of the studies and hospitalization (if needed) to the patient; it is hoped that this method will give the 'testee' an appreciation of the high cost of medical care and the necessity for being thoughful in the way he/she orders laboratory studies. The denouement of each case discusses the differential diagnosis as well as a logical approach to the diagnosis. Each case has with it a current (1992) bibliography. The stacks are designed for medical students, house officers and physicians but may be of interest to others. Clare N. Shumway, M.D. (Clarens)

If you have the measures (length, width, height, radius, or diameter) of a figure; eg. a cube, and you don't know the formula for the volume of a cube, this program will show you a picture a cube, calculate your measures according to the formula and present you with the correct answer, and it teaches you how to do the formula yourself. title: Solving Algabraic Equations
description: Equation Solvent was created by me, Richard Piotter. I have been programing since I was 10. I learned BASIC by using the manuals for my OLD TRS-80 Color Computer 2. I learned parts of the HyperTalk scripting language by examining the scripts of stacks and tring things out with the message box. Later, the computer applications teacher at my school, Thomas Hartwig, let me borrow a HyperTalk sripting reference book. Now I work with HyperCard all the time. Now I work with animation, sound, speech synthesis, and color, just to name a few. I developed Equation Solvent to help me with my geometry. The first study hall I got, I wrote out a BASIC program to use with my TRS-80. Soon after, I made a HyperCard stack that could calculate complimentary and supplimentary angles. My next version included the ability to find the value of X when the equations equaled each other. In the next version I made, you could access the Calculator Application with a button, IF it was in the apple menu. It could also find the value of X when the sums of the two equations equal what is entered in the sums box. This version has a built in calculator and is protected so kids can't accidentally mess any thing up. Equation Solvent 2.1 is a must for people who have trouble with Geometry or algebra. *** You'll have to expiriment with the step if increase or decrease number. I can usually get by with .25 in the box. If you don't get an answer with that setting, try smaller numbers. It will take longer, but you should get some kind of answer. If you like it, I would appreciate it if you sent me 5 dollars. I also want your questions and comments. Also, when I create HyperCard stacks, I make them simple and easy to use. I am always trying to make my stacks better and better, and those of you who send in the 5 dollars will be the first to get info about new things. If you can send in your screen name, I might be able to send e-mail that would describe what I have. You can send a check to me at: Richard Piotter 301 Maple SE Sleepy Eye, MN 56085 If you want to send me questions or comments, you can send them to me at the same address or send them through America Online. Use the screenName RichFiles. I also checked the stack for viruses using Sam Intercept.

title: Culture Interchange Square: Nigiri-zushi (Japan) 1.2
description: What is "CULTURE INTERCHANGE" Square? Sushi is a typical food in Japanese culture. Although I'm not a professional in the sushi world, I've had sushi in my home and sushi bars. As an typical Japanese native, I decided to introduce this field of my homeland on the Internet as a program. The reason why is printed below. (I had to study this field for content and discovered I knew little of our culture.) If the people of the world could introduce/exchange/learn cultural things to/from others, it's a great thing, I think. Of course, such a thing is possible to do via home pages on the Internet combined with the powerful search engines that have been developed. However, if there were a directory (folder) that controlled by minimum rules, a world wide archive, it would be more useful and helpful for viewers. Much like an encyclopedia. Therefore I named this program's subject line a title that conforms to the following; "the title of my product (My country, province or state) - CULTURE INTERCHANGE Square ." If you're making culture introductory/interchange programs to post on the InfoMac archives, would you name your program's subject line that conforms to the " title (your country, province, or state) - CULTURE INTERCHANGE Square" rule? As the proposer, I had to establish the last notes "CULTURE INTERCHANGE Square." It was absolutely necessary to give it a proper first name for obvious reasons. Fuminori Takeda

title: Font Cataloging Utility

This is a plain vanilla stack (no colors or fancy graphics), but it is both useful and easy to use. Care has been taken to make all of the operations clear from the first time you open the stack. If you use a utility like Suitcase or Extensions Manager, you might keep all of your font samples in one stack with labeled dividers between the categories, or you may create separate stacks for each category of your fonts (Serif, Headline, etc.). You can generate printed samples of the fonts in 5 different sizes with 3 font samples printed per page. (Save paper by easily arranging to print on both sides.) By the way, there are several other HC stacks in the libraries that perform much the same task (search by keyword font to locate them). title: CreySys
description: CreySys I comes with 3 primary features: CreyFile This is CreySys I's star program. It allows a user to manage his own file structure within HyperCard. It can manage folders, documents, and HyperCard stacks. CreyWord This is a Word Processor for HyperCard. It allows a user to save fully formatted documents, readable by CreyWord. It also includes a CreyWord Viewer, for readonly access to CreyWord files. CreyTalk CreyTalk is a file-control language. The stack is a development environment. CreyTalk, when used efficiently, can knock hours off of the production time of programs that use extensive file-handling. It is incredibly versatile and powerful. CreySys I also comes with 2 free extras: Credits Screen Builder This program creates dynamic scrolling credits, with little or no need for modification. No programming required. Simply fill in a few blanks and click "OK". The screen is transferred directly to the clipboard, where it can be inserted anywhere with "Paste". CreyKey CreyKey is a text editor with the powerful capability to switch between a CreyFile interface, or a standalone Finder interface, with nothing but the touch of a button. This program is ideal for a CreyFile replacement text editor, and was designed with CreyFile specifically in mind. If you don't register, the programs will permanently disable themselves after 60 uses (Accesses). The data files created by the demos will not be affected, so you can keep all of the data when you update to the full version.

It helps kids study their spelling words. It displays the word for a short period of time. and speaks it (optional). The child is then prompted to enter the spelling of the word. If the word was spelled correctly, a message is displayed noting that it was correct, plays a sound (also optional) and then continues to the next word. If the word was spelled incorrectly, it displays a message saying so and plays a sound (again, optional). Before continuing to the next word it displays the correct spelling of the word and how the user spelled it so that the user can compare their attempt with the correct spelling. Missed words are presented again until the user spells them correctly. At the end, an analysis of the student's progress is displayed.

title: Paper Chaste 0.7
description: Paper Chaste is a tool to aid teachers in analyzing writing samples in the Roman alphabet. This HyperCard stack calculates the frequency of recorded mistakes so that you can guide students toward correcting the most common mistakes found within their writing as a group, and it does a simple statistical analysis--mean, median, variance, standard deviation, and standardization [z = (X-Mean)/Standard Deviation] of final "gravity of error" totals that are then transformed into grades/marks on an alterable (mean) scale. The mistakes themselves must be entered into a list *by the user*, where they can then be repeatedly loaded into a student report by clicking on the line containing the error. P_C comes with a list of around 90 grammatical errors (in French) and over 20 student reports to serve as data examples. The program is in some ways just a shell that you have to fill in with your own expertise, i.e., P_C does NOT analyze the writing for you; it analyzes the mistakes that you record. Aside from the analysis, the other primary objective of the program is to more actively involve the students in correcting their own mistakes by providing reports to guide them. Documentation is limited to the FAQ file that is bundled with the program.

title: Script Optimizer 1.0.1
description: Script Optimizer 1.0.1 is a HyperCard stack that can analyze other stacks' scripts and perform one to three optimizations on them: * keyword abbrevation * comment removal * global checking Keyword Abbreviation HyperCardUs long-format keywords, such as 'background' can be abbreviated to their shortest equivalent, such as 'bg.' (Any keywords within quotation marks are left as-is.) Comment Removal Removes lines that begin with '--', and 'embedded comments' within a line, such as 'on mouse up -- user stops'. (Comment characters inside quotation marks are not removed.) Global Checking Often during the development cycle of a stack, some global variables remain within handlers, even though they are not used any longer. These unused globals can be removed. (Global variables that are declared within quotation marks are not checked.) Script Optimizer also generates detailed reports on all optimization that it makes to a stack.

title: Cyphercard 1.0a
description: Cyphercard is a simple but effective tool (a Hypercard stack) for encoding and decoding text documents. It uses no complex math, but simply encodes one document (the target) by using a second document as a reference document or a 'one time scratch pad', as they call it in the cypher biz. Cyphercard does not just use simple pointers into the reference document, but modulates those pointers using offsets which are also taken from that document. It uses various other encoding techniques as well. None of this is complicated or tricky. It doesn't need to be. The security of the code is not compromised at all by knowing exactly how it was coded. All the security comes from the fact that the code depends on the reference document and the password. The upshot is that anyone who knows which reference document and password to use can decode any document that was encoded with that reference document and password. Anyone who does not know that document and password will not be able to decode the target document. Cyphercard requires a PowerPC Macintosh and Hypercard 2.4.

title: Movies 6.1
description: A HyperCard Home Video Organizer This stack was started a couple of years ago by Andrew Stanton at Pixar. He wanted to be able to catalog and organize his laserdiscs. I started with his basic stack and learned HyperHacking while I added some features. We and some of our friends at Pixar have been using it for over a year (you'll note the Feb 1993 date on the release) and it's been quite useful. Features at a glance: * Keep track of all of your discs and tapes. * Remembers to whom you lent out that video, and when. * Lets you search by criteria such as Director, running time, or by features such as letterboxing, supplements, etc. * Calculates the total value of your collection for insurance purposes. The stack comes with one bogus card which is not a movie, but a set of instructions and examples. Detailed help is available by pulling down the Movies menu and selecting Preferences. There you will find a Help icon. The Movies Stack is c Craig Good. It is FreeWare, but is not in the public domain. It may be freely distributed as long as it is intact and unmodified. If you like it, or if you have suggestions, contact me at good@pixar.com or NM8N GUY (America On Line)

I also added suitable clip art and ,of course, the music and lyrics to the song. It is a beautiful song of praise to God and my favorite, which I would like to share with anyone who is interested. Click the speaker icon to begin. It puts up a panel of four phonograms then pronounces one of them. If the user clicks on the correct one, he gets a coin. If not, a word using the phonogram is displayed, [and optionally spoken.] The original B&W Hypercard version of this program has a great track record at getting youngsters turned on to the mechanics of reading. It might be even better in color. No warranties of any kind. May be included on CD-ROMS.

One template is included that will print name cards for use at conferences. If you can read HyperTalk and can write a little PostScript, you can use this example to create your own templates. title: MacElements Utilities v1.03
description: TYPE: Freeware Stack SYSTEM 7: Compatible MacElements Utilities is a HyperCard 2.x stack which calculates: element percentages isotope patterns yields of chemical reactions for given chemical formulae. It should therefore be useful in any chemical laboratory. All the user has to do is enter the formulae and weights (or number of mmols) of starting material and product. Formulae are correctly parsed and displayed with numeric subscripts (not common in HyperCard). The yield is calculated automatically. Additionally, element percentages for the chemical formulae are automatically calculated and displayed. A short report is automatically generated in Royal Society of Chemistry or American Chemical Society experimental section punctuation styles. The user may also elect to calculate the isotope pattern of chemical formulae. Some 50 different pseudo- elements such as Me, Ph, Cp, etc. are allowed in chemical formulae. Note: This stack is a small component of the MacElements package, a soon-to-be-published (really !) HyperCard based periodic table database and utilities package with the ability to export data, to plot graphs of properties of the elements, and lots of other things. It also does reaction yields and element percentages, of course! Details available from the author (preferably by e-mail please).

title: megaSort1.1.SIT
description: ----------------- Version 1.1 of megaSort. Corrects problem with using words as keys. MegaSort allows you to sort a container with multiple keys, ascending or descending, as ASCII, number, date or International keys using any character as delimiters. Any character can be used as a record or field delimiter. This allows you to sort on chunks within chunks (e.g. char 3 to 7 of item 3 of each line). megaSort is an XFCN that you copy to your stack using ResEdit, ResCopy or another resource copy program.

title: Munchkin Matters 1.0
description: Munchkin Matters 1.0 This stack is directed primarily for children between 3 and 8 years of age. It is intended to help children learn the basic alphabet and begin putting together their own words. There are 8 distinctive areas included, of which most are learning areas, and some are just for fun. Picture and word association play a major role in the basic format of the stack. Speech has been installed throughout to say the letters, put words to pictures and allow kids to build their own words and then have the computer speak that word for them. Picture-word match game that rewards higher levels of achievement and attempts to help when the learning level needs improvement. Game and fun areas are in place to help with computer and mouse skills as well as keeping things interesting. Paint is included that is quite fun and will keep even the adults sitting at the Mac for hours. A two player game for developing thought and strategy, which includes spoken help and spoken prompts to get them started. An area for saving their art produced in paint, which can be used in a slide show type of format. Auto painted graphics for the smallest kids that might not be ready to paint on their own. It's really pretty cool and I'll bet you adults will click these buttons a few times. Useful at schools, day care and home. ShareWare. Scanned with Disinfectant for any known viruses. Well worth download time - take a look - I think you'll like it. (Staff note: This stack requires Macintalk in your system folder. It is incompatible with 32-bit operation.)

title: Nova Scotia & New Brunswick
description: This is a stack about the history, economy, and geography of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick, Canada. I did it as a school project 3 years ago in the 6th grade. Use it to plan a vacation. Or do a school project. Or just enjoy. It has a nifty sound of my voice.

title: NewFileName XFCN 1.0

title: PowerList 2.0.1
description: ----------------- PowerList is a XFCN that brings up a dialog box containing a scrollable list of information stored in a container. PowerList includes several features missing in other similar externals. PowerList has the ability to accept input by line (return-delimited), by item (comma-delimited), by STR or STR# resource, or by any text file. PowerList will optionally sort the list before displaying it in the dialog. PowerList accepts several different 'parameters' which determine varying selection criteria of the list. It will also accept a prompt string for display in its dialog box. PowerList allows you change the font of the dialog box to any currently available font, and the dialog itself can be placed anywhere on the screen. PowerList 2.0 introduces a new and exciting trend in how the user determines all of the available features operate and how the dialog box looks on the screen. The user now supplies a 'configuration string' to PowerList that contains several 'keywords' that determine the exact function of each individual feature. Fixes/Changes in version 2.0.1 - PowerList would hang when a number was passed to the sort and/or chopStrings keyword. - Dialog boxes could not be positioned correctly. Note that there is still a slight variance in positioning dialogs. - When pressing cancel or after pressing OK without choosing a cell, PowerList would sometimes return garbage. - A scroll bar is no longer displayed in a list that doesn't require one.

It will hopefully speed up the process of creating stacks, by providing a standard background with separate windows for text and graphics. It has been used extensively in education, ranging from 7th grade thru Grad-school. Students would typically create multimedia notebooks with text appearing on the left and illustrations on the right. It has been used in all content areas, from Science and Mathematics to Art and Literature. It has also been used by educators in writing workshops. In adition to its educational applications, SODA FlipBook has enjoyed the experimentation of creative artists. SODA Flip has several paint palettes for making stack design quicker. It contains a sample palette from SODAColor, the scriptless multimedia solution from Runfast, Inc. FlipBook Color WILL run just as well on black&white Macs. Besides, there are many excellent sorting XFCNs available already. Non-commercial use of this XFCN is free. =George=

The Herald Angels Sing -Came Upon the Midnight Clear -Jolly Old Saint Nicholas -O Come, All Ye Faithful -Silent Night -We Three Kings of Oreint Are Enjoy the carols in this stack, or copy the buttons or scripts to your own creations to liven them up. title: HyperGrapher Calculator 1.0b
description: TYPE: Freeware Stack SYSTEM 7: Compatible Author's email address: bscott@east.east-slc.edu I wrote this simple graphic calculator stack because I couldn't afford Mathematica, nor could I buy one of those spiffy PowerMacintoshes with the new 3D graphic calculator on it. So, for those of you who already have one of the above items, then you don't need this stack. However, if you're just an average student-user like myself, read on. Some helpful hints: 1: Type the equations into the fields in HyperCard's standard form. X3 would be typed 'X^3' (minus the apostrophes). 2: All odd exponent powers are rounded off (absolute value)x To make up for this, multiply the variable times itself raised to one less power. 3: The four small fields control the setting of the X-axis and Y-axis, much like the RANGE values found in graphic calculators. The X-off and Y-off are the offset, or how many points (not pixels) to the right, left, up, or down of the origin (the REAL origin). The Xscl and Yscl variables set how many pixels are to be places between each point on the grid. So, if you wanted to 'zoom in', you'd enter in a big number. If you wanted to zoom out, you'd enter in a small number (at least 1). It's not exactly the best screen to zoom in and out of, but it's bigger than some of those hand-held calculator screens, and better resolution too! 4: The Equation Field is merely a field I created to store all my conic section formulas (solved for x). You can modify or copy from it any way you'd like, and I hope you DO personalize it. (Just make sure you put the previous equations BACK if you distribute your working copy.)

title: HyperSavvy HOT for scripters v1.
description: Many of the scripts in HyperSavvy have already proven themselves out in the field, in other stacks, enjoying a minimum of a year's worth of debugging and polishing before ever appearing in HyperSavvy; so it's highly unlikely you'll encounter a problem with them. This stack is NOT PROTECTED, and is set to USERLEVEL 5. So you have full access to everything (so long as you possess the full-blown HyperCard application). This allows you to use HyperSavvy as a 'template' stack- transform it directly into your own custom stacks- or borrow bits and pieces instead, as preferred. Anyone may use these scripts (AND the custom navigation palette) in their own shareware and commercial stacks, with no more obligation than plainly stating within those stacks the source of the code and palette. Major scripting techniques presented in HyperSavvy (there's lots of minor ones not listed here) include the following: 1. Central control over stack navigation that ties together the cursor/arrow keys, Go menu commands, custom menu commands, keyboard menu shortcuts, and an attractive custom navigation palette into a single system. This allows you to change all stack navigation operations with just the tweak of a few central script handlers. 2. Your own 'About [filename]...' under the Apple menu, and 'Quit [filename]' under the File menu, and a custom '[filename]' menu, easily customized within MINUTES for your own stacks. 3. Comprehensive modifications of HyperCard menus on demand- menu AND menu item deletions, disablings, enablings, breaks, and redirections of commands- whatever you require for your own project. 4. A fast and elegant PopUp menu (in pure HyperTalk) which changes 'on-the-fly' to present different options for different cards. 5. An exhaustive trap script for all HyperCard menu calls- simply insert your own custom scripts for any menu item you desire. This gives you absolute control of every menu command in a stack (and so is great when you want to protect your stack from hackers with a few special tricks of your own). 6. Full custom menu and palette wrap, and transparent userLevel changes, within multi-stack environments. This greatly reduces the chance of script errors, while also making your own stacks behave much more professionally under complex conditions. You (and your stack's users) will be delighted. 7. A wide range of ask and answer dialog boxes, from simple to complex. 8. Three different styles of progress gauge dialogs, including the standard bar, and new choices of page and circular styles, all equipped with Abort buttons (pure HyperTalk). 9. A versatile scrolling list dialog with click-to-choose and multiple function buttons. 10. 'Find...' and 'Find Next' scripts complete with a full range of contingency dialogs already built-in, ready and waiting to be pasted into your own stack. 11. Background processing for HyperCard, with examples providing time and date readouts for users, and a trap script that'll tell you everytime a user begins or quits typing into a field. 12. Ways to keep tabs on the size of any script or text field in a stack, and intercept and deal with HyperCard script errors in a way invisible to end-users. Why spend months trying to gather up and comprehend all these techniques separately, with little or no help, and then spend more months attempting to make them all work together, when you can just download HyperSavvy today? Become an overnight expert in HyperCard- by downloading HyperSavvy HOT today.

title: Classroom Manager demo.sea
description: Some Features: - Appointments -- an excellent computerized daytimer with detailed scheduling and overview - Substitute Information -- store in-depth information about you and your classroom for guest teachers - Journal -- a place to keep your professional and personal observations and musings - Parent Conferences -- store detailed information about concerns from all sides, and plans for student success - Student Records -- store valuable information about your students, including address, phone numbers, with plenty of room for notes - Optional Hours -- keep track of your beyond-contract hours easily and accurately, then print them out in June - Textbooks -- automate this annual task by keeping information about your books from year to year - Project Plans -- organize and store details of your best projects - Resources -- arrange your materials by theme, project, or unit - Daily Plans -- keep detailed information about your lesson plans for each day of the year - Weekly Plans -- an overview of your week including activities, homework, and work to collect - Report Printing Style -- printouts and forms that don't look like a picture of the screen from most modules - Navigation and Finding tools -- find information quickly and easily ------ Dancing Rabbit Creations produces educational HyperCard programs and games for teachers, students, parents, and learners of all ages. The stacks are affordably priced, and users can try them before they buy - all are released as shareware, or are available in demonstration versions. Dancing Rabbit Creations is the joint (ad)venture of a classroom teacher and a software tester. NOTE: This stack is a demo version of the software and full working copy.

title: Sounds English 1.16

title: Invisidisk v1.0
description: Need disk security? Need more than just password protection? You need Invisidisk! Invisidisk makes a disk invisable and completly inaccesable to everybody and on every computer. Invisidisk make a disk invisable the same way you do a file or folder, only this can't be undone, even by RESEDIT! The disk becomes completly invisable to all programs (and of course the finder) until made visable again using Invisidisk 1.0. Nobody can use a disk that they can't see. title: AppleEvent XCMDs
description: This stack contains a collection of Apple Event™ XCMDs and handlers which can be used to send Apple events to Apple event-aware applications from HyperCard. It replaces the SendAppleEvent XCMD stack which has been previously distributed via AppleLink and Apple's Developer CD. Object specifiers and AERecords are now supported in this stack! With this HyperCard stack, you may easily create and send Apple events to an application. By reading an application's 'aete' resource, which generates glue routines, you can "test drive" the application by sending it events that it supports. Essentially, this is a mini development environment for scripting other applications using HyperTalk. Since it is entirely implemented in HyperTalk and HyperCard, it may be a little slower for the low-end machines, but nevertheless, it is a rich environment in which new features can easily be added.

Next, you will see the words or phrases to be replaced (wildcards) followed by the words or phrases (substitutions) randomly chosen from their respective cards (Fig. 3) which will replace the wildcards Finally, the finished sentence will be displayed on the screen. title: MerryXmas Virus
description: MerryXmas was the first documented HyperCard virus, and was the only prevalent HyperCard virus for many years. When an infected stack is opened, the virus script copies itself to the Home stack. From there, it spreads to every other stack that is opened. Unopened stacks are not affected, but it still does not take long for most of the stacks on a hard drive to contract the virus. The original intent of MerryXmas was to simply propagate, but because of a typing error, the virus can sometimes copy parts of other stack scripts along with its own virus code. This causes stacks to behave unexpectedly or sometimes to error. In certain circumstances, MerryXmas also causes HyperCard to quit (but not crash) without any notification. (from https://www.hyperactivesw.com/hypercard/virus1.html ) This stack includes both a stack to infect the Home stack (in the disk 'Virus') and the tool 'MerryXmas Vaccine' (in the disk 'disk') that can detect and disinfect the virus. Please note that the emulator is an isolated environment and therefore all changes (and infected stacks) are lost when the emulator is closed. The came cannot be said if you download this stack to run elsewhere - extreme caution is advised! Particular thanks to Space Rogue of The Whacked Mac Archives for tracking down this virus so it can be preserved in the HyperCard collection.

title: PickupBackground XCMD
description: ----------------- HyperCard 2 format but converted from original HyperCard 1.2.5 stack. WHAT IS IT? When are backgrounds the same? Ever get frustrated when copying a card from one place to another, a card which supposedly has the 'same' background, only to find that a new background was created? HyperCard has a quite literal view of when backgrounds are the same, namely, when their byte counts & such are identical. This XCMD will let you paste card-specific information (card picture, field text, card fields, card buttons and card script) 'into' another background. In effect this lets you change which background a certain card belongs to. Extremely useful in merging multiple stacks or backgrounds together. USAGE 1) Go to the card you wish to move to a different background. 2) Choose 'Copy Card' (or 'Cut Card') from the Edit menu. 3) Go to the background which you want the copied card to belong to. 4) Hold down the option key while selecting 'Paste Card' from the Edit menu. or Call the XCMD directly as explained in the notes.

title: TextMuncher 0.93
description: From the Help field: TextMuncher is a text processor. I created it because I found that I sometimes needed to edit or format text in ways not easy in a word processor. TextMuncher not only cleans up raw ASCII text, but it also allows you to do such things as: change all words which begin with 'pre' to upper case, or delete any lines which contain 'http://', or set each word which immediately precedes 'Instructions:' to a given textFont, textSize, and textStyle. Capabilities: *** Operations: clean up ASCII text by taking out EOL's, replacing double en-dashes with em-dashes, smart-quoting, eliminating superfluous hyphens, etc. Also delete, replace, sort, change case, set text styles, and extract specified chunks of text to the clipboard. *** Operate on a selected block of text or on all lines/words/characters/strings which contain/don't contain/equal/begin with/end with/follow/precede a specified string, in the entire text or in any selected block of text. *** Define any combination of textFont, textSize, and textStyle as a named style, and apply it, via a pop-up list, to selected text or to all lines/words/... etc., which contain/don't contain/... etc. Like a limited version of word-processor styling, but with much more flexibility in applying styles automatically to specified pieces of text. *** Undo any of the above operations. *** Export the formatted text as an RTF file, which can be opened by most word processors. Limitations: *** Only works (for now) on text files less than 30K. *** Very slow on large searches. (That's Hypercard - powerful but slow.) *** Somewhat fussy about its operations. However, for certain procedures, it's still lots faster than doing it by hand in a word processor! DL this stack and keep it on hand. If in two months you haven't used it, then trash it. But I bet in that time you'll have come across at least one situation when you'll find it very handy...

title: 1st. Law of Thermodynamics

They have been tested with Freshman students and received enthusiastic reviews. The basic ideas of system, surroundings, energy transfers (heat and work), internal energy, measurement of internal energy change (or E, if you like), enthalpy, measurement of enthalpy change, relation between enthalpy change and internal energy change, thermochemical equations and Hess's law are all covered. Product will include full documentation. See also the demos 'Chemistry Tutoring' (which outlines the CheMentor series in more detail) and 'The Chemistry Toolkit' - also the Shareware product 'The Molar Mass Calculator' - all of which are available from AOL (use Command-5 or the GoTo menu (File Search)).

title: Proportion Wheel 2.0
description: Proportion Wheel allows the user to properly scale photos for publication. It doesn't matter whether you use picas, inches or even mixed measures --although this version will not convert picas to inches. To use Proportion Wheel, press the New Photo button. It will ask you the original width, new width, original length and new length of the photo you are measuring. It is important that you fill in only three of the four required numbers. To determine the percentage and either new length or width of photo, press the Go Figure button. If you filled in all four blanks, Go Figure will put a new number in the New Width box. To change a single number in the Width or Length boxes (you can change one without affecting the other numbers), simply click on the one you want to change, enter the new number and press Go Figure. Remember that before you press the Go Figure button, one of the four Width and Length boxes must be empty. To convert fractions to their decimal equivalent, or vice-versa, click on Converter and enter either the fraction or the decimal. You can convert fractions as small as 1/64th of an inch. To get rid of the Converter box, simply click on it.

title: free journal stack
description: This is just the journal from the IF MONKS HAD MACS... monastery library. I have never uploaded it separately before, but I noticed there was a lot of interest in a crippled shareware journal here. Even though it is very old I think anyone interested in the other journal, ought to have a look at this one. This is not crippled, [or shareware] but for the price he is asking I'll send you the entire monastery library and register you as a supporter of our 40 megabyte riverTEXT collection which will be released next year. MacDigest wrote: 'Just under 3 megabytes in size, IF MONKS HAD MACS... contains enough ideas and entertainment (including the best personal journal system this writer has ever seen) to be enjoyed conceivably for a lifetime.' It will work just fine in HyperCard 2, but only AFTER you convert it. title: Life Expectancy Calculator 1.0
description: OK, I'll admit it...life expectancy sounds like a pretty dull subject. But Life Expectancy Calculator actually makes it fun! This entertaining and informative program will ask for your age, race and sex. Then, after a drum roll, it will display your life expectancy (based on government statistics). Next, it will draw a bar graph comparing your current age to your expected life span. Click a button and you'll hear harp music as an angel presents a humorous 'Countdown to Eternity' clock that counts down the time you have left! In addition, you can read a collection of humor and quotations about aging, including quotes from Jerry Seinfeld, George Burns, Bill Cosby and Cary Grant. Finally, you can learn how to 'beat the odds' and live a longer and healthier life. This program contains nice graphics and sound effects, and is free.

title: Skytalks - Deconstructing DeFeNeStRaTe.C - Slides
description: In 2012 a hacker in Cambodia gained unauthorized access to a mainframe in Sweden. This breach has been talked about in depth by this speaker and many others. One little known aspect of the breach was the release of a program on pastebin called 'DeFeNeStRaTe.C'. This program uses a buffer overflow attack against an APF authorized Unix (USS) program: IOELMD10. In this session we will walk through exactly what the attack does, how it worked, a demo against an APF authorized program in Unix on z/OS and how you can detect and protect APF authorized Unix programs. If you wish to prepare the source code is available here: https://github.com/mainframed/logica/blob/master/DeFeNeStRaTe.C

title: Recipe Maker 1.0s demo.sea
description: This is a program that I wrote for my step-mom, who loves recipes, to keep up with them in a database program with HyperCard. This program allows you to store as many recipes as you may need, print them, absolutely do whatever you want to do with them. As a cheap shareware program, costing only $5, it is cheap databasing, without too much effort. It has 4 main fields in it along with an easy interface. It begins with a stack that gives instructions on usage and buying and registering. The registered version will allow you to make new recipe entries. The demo contains one entry that you can use if you want to. It is an example of how it shall work. The first field - Food name. The description of the target food. The second - Ingredients. This is where you put the necessary ingredients. The third - Method. How you use the ingredients. The fourth - Tools. This describes what you will need (crock pot, oven, etc.) If you register the program, you will be sent a cardboard disk mailer with a high-density disk that contains 3 files: a readme, a .sea file for HyperCard 2.x users, and a stack for non-HyperCard users. If you want 2 800k floppies, please specify this when you purchase. Please check it out. Checked for Mac & HC viruses with Virex 5.6.

title: MiniTool Palette
description: The MiniTool is a small 2 1/2' by 1/2' palette with nine icons. Combining modifier keys with single clicks allows you to perform over 50 different navigation and authoring tasks. The palette is completely 'self-contained' and requires no additional stack scripting; just copy its three resources into your Home stack, type 'palette miniTool' in the Message Box, and away you go. Using combinations of modifier keys and single clicks, MiniTool can take you to the 1st card, previous card, recent card, 1st bkgnd, previous bkgnd, next card, last card, next bkgnd, last bkgnd, or any stack window. It can open a stack, open the Recent display, push a card, pop a card, start using a stack, stop using a stack, close open windows, hide all HyperCard windows, open ResCopy, open ResEdit, open any other application, open any stack's script, rush you Home, open Home's script, call GetHomeInfo, and do several other things. Stealth mode lets you set lockMessages to true via a modifier key, while you click any palette icon; a real lifesaver when a resumeStack or openCard handler has run amok, or when you're checking out someone else's stack and want to avoid transition effect scripts, 'security' handlers, etc. Of course, if you don't mind adding about five lines of code to your Home stack script, you can also use the MiniShow XCMD, which keeps the palette 'pinned' above the center of the current stack. (Of course, it's smart enough to take the title bar and menu bar into account, and it never lets the palette become lost chasing off-screen card window locations). Complete documentation is in the stack, and the palette displays a comprehensive Help window with a single click. The stack, palette, and MiniShow external require HC 2.0 or higher. A couple of bonus externals that work with HC 1.2.2 or higher are also included. Grokware -- if you 'get' it, you can keep it.

title: Scroll from the keyboard
description: The card scripts in this stack enable you to scroll text in a specified field from the keyboard. The 1st card uses the Up and Down arrow keys, while the next card is geared toward older Macs that don't have arrow keys. You can retain at least some of the quick-look aspect by enabling users to scroll text with a single key. The up and down arrow keys used alone scroll text by 'screenfuls' the amount of text visible at any one time in the field. Pressing Option-Up arrow takes you back to the beginning of the text, and pressing Shift-Down arrow or Shift-Up arrow scrolls text down or up by one line. The script enables the scrolling by measuring the height of the field and the height of text in the field, and by calculating how much text is scrolled 'off the top' of the field. It seems to work well with any field, but try to size your fields so that they show full lines of text. If you size a field so that the bottom line is half hidden, for instance, you might not get consistent scrolling.

The StackTools menu contains 15 submenus, each of which accesses a particular utility or group of related utilities. The StackTools menu is installed in the menu bar by clicking on a button, and may be removed at any time.

title: Softcourse System 1.22 BETA
description: Shareware, $20 Softcourse was originally designed to assist college-level students in learning information presented to them through video telecourses. It affords these students an opportunity to experience a more structured learning environment (which the television courses lacked) without confining a student to a set class schedule or instructor. The technology of television freed the student from having to attend lectures while allowing them to still see them; Computer technology allows the student a more involved environment for learning things with the same flexibility that the television course material offers. Softcourse is a HyperCard stack that is already half-written for you. This half-written stack is laid out in a way so that any information put into it will be in an accessible and learnable format. All of the heavy technical-whizbang stuff has been figured out for you. Softcourse is meant to let you focus on what you do the best: sharing the knowledge you have. In many ways, it is like a form which you can fill your very own information into, doodle a little here and there, and feed it through a machine that makes it a finished product. You are, of course, still very responsible for the content of your material, but the stress of presenting that material in this new way (on the computer) is lessened. The reason for that is (hopefully) because you don't have to worry about much else. What Softcourse offers to you, the instructor, is the freedom to construct a piece of information-rich courseware that not only allows the student to engage in active, self-directed exploration of material, but also automatically remediates. That is, it alters how it presents material to the student as it finds out what portions of the material they are having difficulty with, on the fly. Many courseware program force learners to absorb the material in an almost linear fashion, and become limiting, binding, and impractical. Softcourse attempts to be different. It provides you the opportunity to positively utilize HyperCard without learning all of its intracies. On the other hand, the more you learn about HyperCard, the more you can make your courseware interactive and customized. You want to have a certain piece of material jump to another stack, do something, and jump back? Softcourse allows you to modify and add to any portion of it, until your courseware is just right. In essence, it lets you concentrate on the content of a stack, while Softcourse software concetrates on the function. From that end, your students get your best material, a step-by-step list of things to explore, structured tests set up to your specifications, auto-remediation, and it still lets them access any piece of information, look at any test's results, take any test, or check what their list of things to do it at any time. Pretty Spiffy, eh? Megs

title: Domestic Violence Assessment
description: The Domestic Violence Hypercard stack has been developed to assist mental health professionals, attorneys and investigators working perpetrators and victims of domestic violence and child abuse. The Domestic Violence stack is an overview stack of the assessment and risk determination process. This stack is freeware and is available at no charge. This series also includes three add-on stacks for conducting assessments with either victims or perpetrators; the Domestic Violence Inventory, the Child Abuse Inventory and the Domestic Violence Risk Assessment. The Domestic Violence and Child Abuse Inventories can be utilized to take comprehensive histories with both victims and perpetrators of violence. Summaries can be printed and inserted into the chart for easy review. These stacks also provide a numerical breakdown of the frequency of high, moderate and low level violent acts. The Domestic Violence Risk Assessment stack provides both a summary and recommendations that guide the professional in decision-making with regard to risk prevention and treatment planning. These stacks are shareware and are for sale at $20.00 each or all three for $50.00. These stacks will be available for Windows/DOS users in 1998. Visit my web site for further information at http://members.aol.com/dsonkin/index.html. ------------------------------------------------- Checked for viruses with Disinfectant and Vaccine.

The CharToHex XFCN and the HexToChar XFCN are Copyright 1992 - Barry Dobson - workingweekend productions. In this way, multiple connections may be made between topics, subtopics, related topics, etc. and information may be organized and accessed according to the needs of the moment. If you're a student and you use a PowerBook, try taking lecture notes or reading notes in this stack. If you then attach appropriate tags to the cards, you'll be able to use the tags and searching capabilities to review your notes in a very flexible way.
