SMPTE is a time code used for synchronizing sounds with video,etc. A very informative stack that looks pretty slick as well! If you've ever seen one of Joe's stacks before, you know you don't want to miss this!

title: Ask/Answer Scripter
description: Ask/Answer Scripter allows you to to create and format either dialog window in a slightly smaller than real-life box. Choose and name the number of buttons required (or type a default text prompt in the Ask dialog), choose the buttons you would like to attach if...then statements to, then click 'Write Script'. Scripter will generate the script to produce the dialog, place the script in a field, and display the dialog for your inspection. This utility would be real handy for new scripters but, would be useful to anyone who wants to avoid a zillion trips to the script editor to pretty-up dialogs. Lots of examples available from the 'Ideas' button. tomF

description: Discusses elementary photography procedures using overautomated camera; useful for new photographers less exposed (*grin*) to technicalities; v1.5 includes camera simulator for visualizing depth of field with varying lighting conditions, focal length, and film speed; requires Hypercard2.1 or later.

description: Baby monitor is a HyperCard stack designed to help you keep track of a young infant's bodily functions - urination, defecation, and feeding. Or, to keep things light, peeing, pooping and eating. It is designed to run on an old Mac you keep next to the baby's things, so you can leave it up all the time and use it easily as you go by. It is sized to fit a 640 by 480 screen.

title: BIBLIOPHILE Book Collection/Journal Citation Stack

title: CompareString()
description: This is an external function (xfcn) that takes 2 strings as parameters and returns TRUE if they match and FALSE if not. The comparison is case sensitive so 'A' does not equal 'a'. The comparison includes the entire ascii character set, including invisible characters such as space, tab and the like. The stack also demonstrates the difference in performance between pure Hypertalk, compiled Hypertalk, and optimized code using CompileIt! Checked with Disinfectant 2.5.1 and found to be clean and pure. -----Comment from SIG Leader---If you do a lot of CaSe SeNsItIvE comparisons between two text strings, you need this XFCN.

title: Growth Records.sit
description: This is my first HC stack. One of the most difficult problems for physicians who deal with children is evaluation of their growth pattern and to determine whether that growth pattern is normal or abnormal. Since approximately five percent of all children are either too small or too tall, abnormalities in growth are relatively common and yet are all to frequently not recognized for several years. This HC stack permits the rapid plotting and recognition of abnormal growth patterns. This HC stack is free for the casual user...like those of you who want to know whether your kid is growing ok. Please feel free to distribute this software to your friends; however, if you use it for professional use, please request a copy from the author. I have not included a number of functions which the professional might find useful. If you are interested in seeing a more complete stack or if you would like some special implementation established, contact me by E-Mail. Steven Seelig M.D., Ph.D.

title: HyperEquation v2.0

title: IconStack 1.7.6
description: IconStack is the ultimate HyperCard B/W icon library by Geoff Williams, the author of other notable HyperCard stacks such as Bomb Hunter, Pig Professional, ArtBlox and the HyperMagic series (for magicians only). This handy HyperCard stack contains over 100 screens (cards) totaling MORE THAN 7000 icon-size black & white pictures (32 pixels by 32 pixels) of almost every conceivable form. Users can modify, copy and create their own icons for use in graphics programs, System 7 desktop icons, HyperCard buttons, ResEdit, etc. Icons can easily be added to this library. Very easy to use, IconStack icons can be accessed from the main Index Card or by searching for a user-definable keyword (many keywords may be associated with each card). To copy an icon, just click on it. A Paint Area is available for modifying/creating icons (modifying/creating icons on the main cards is discouraged to prevent mishaps to the other icons on the card). Centering Guides make exact placement of icons in the icon grid quick and simple. Keyboard shortcuts, for most operations, are shown onscreen.

title: TitleBar 1.1 XCMD.SIT

Great little XCMD if you use hypercard to give presentations on a MacII. TitleBar accepts one parameter: toggle -- toggles the titlebar hide -- hides the titlebar show -- shows the titlebar -- same as toggle

title: Too Much Joy
description: This stack is all about that great 'alternative' rock band you all know and love, Too Much Joy. Celebrated for its crunching power pop-rock riffs and clever, intelligent lyrics, TMJ (as its fans refer to the band) has received rave reviews in Rolling Stone, Spin, Billboard, Entertainment Weekly, People, The New York Times, The Village Voice, Tower Records' Pulse, Request, The Hard Report, CashBox and a ton of other magazines. In fact, CashBox named them one of the top bands of the '90s. Clearly, if you haven't heard of TMJ before, now is the time to catch on to them quick! And this stack can help you do that.... The stack contains a detailed history of the band, (including lots of dirt on its legal problems with Bozo The Clown and being arrested for protesting censorship). There is also an annotated discography, song analysis, soundbites, over 100 pop-up fields, information on how to join JoyBuzzer, TMJ's official fan club, and lots more! This stack is the 'Clean Version,' meaning that some of the -ahem- 'language' has been cleaned up in order to meet AOL's standards; also a soundbite from the rarity, 'Rap Like Mine,' has been removed (a move which saved 51K, incidentally, making this a shorter download). Otherwise, the stack is the same as 'regular' version. This stack is Freeware; it is also Joinware-if you like it, do yourself a favor and join JoyBuzzer (you'll get an fan-club-only, blue-vinyl EP of unreleased TMJ songs and four issues of JoyBuzzer Fanzine, which has gotten rave reviews of its own in Tower Records' Pulse, Factsheet Five and Yellow Pills). Like I said, information on how to join is in the stack. Smash a glass, and en-Joy the stack- Clive Young JoyBuzzer

title: Zip Codes 2.6.sea
description: ----------------- This stack allows you to search for any zip for any city. You may search by zip code, by city, or by a text string. Also allows editing of the database to enter new cities or codes. Works better with faster machines but still finds zips for a search on city name in 10 seconds or less on a Mac Plus. Well worth the download, and I believe bug free thanks to responses by other users of previous versions. Thanks to them! This is shareware.

title: Disk-it.sit
description: This stack is very useful and easy to use. Basically what this stack does is keep a catalog of your floppy disks. All you do is create a new card by pressing the 'New Card' button. The underneath 'Set Disk Size' is there merely to calculate the free size left on your disk. The default setting is for 800k disks. If you want to catalog a 400k disk just press the button next to the field. If you don't know what size disk you are using, leave it set to 800 - no big deal. Next press the 'Catalog Disk' button. This brings up a dialog box requesting you insert a floppy disk - it does the rest! When the catolog is finished your disk will pop out. Just follow the same procedure to catolog your other disks. If your disks change information a lot, go back to the card which you want to re-catalog and start the same procedure over again. The fields will be written-over by the new information, there is no need to erase fields (although you are perfectly welcome to change information by editing fields). The Size of Disk field near the bottom of the card shows your total memory used up on that disk. The Free Size field below it shows the amount of memory left. Also, on the main card, an easy way to go to a particular card or view what cards (disks) you have, just press the 'Table of Contents' button. A dialog will pop up requesting you choose a card to navigate to. If you just want to view the cards (disks) and not go to one then simply choose 'Cancel' and you will be returned to the main card. To print a report with all the contents of your floppies just press the 'Print Report' button. The print dialog box will pop-up and all you have to do is press 'OK'. Normally don't worry about changing anything unless you're not happy with the report.

title: Useful Little Scripts 3
description: *Make all the functions of 'Useful Little Scripts' available all the time, whether or not the stack is open. *Create an 'About. . .' item in the Apple Menu *Assemble a clickable table of contents field *Make a 'Transfer' menu which will allow users to transfer to other applications or files *Put a place marker field in your stack, showing the user where he or she is in the stack *Use a series of PICTs to create a 'working. . .' thermometer to show users how much of a procedure is complete *Copy a subsection of a stack to another stack (for instance, all the people cards of a clip art stack) *Export the text from a stack *Save a script as a text file *Collect all the scripts from a stack *List the field names, numbers, and IDs and put them in a field or text file *Maximize hard drive space by automatically compacting a stack when the ratio of free space/size of the stack reaches 10% *Automatically create a button on the home card leading to your stack *Change ' marks to smart quotes throughout a stack *Make automatic backup copies as part of a script, to make sure you don't irrevocably change a stack ------------- There is no charge for this stack, and you may distribute it freely. All the author asks if you download the stack is email telling him which state you live in. The author is collecting a list of states in which this stack is used--your input is vital!

Each button ask and keeps track of the user's name through the use of a global variable. One button is used for locked media (such as CD-ROM) and the other button is for unlocked media. Each button provides a means of recording information about the user that can be used by your stack to trigger events. Knowledge about HyperTalk and the use of global variables are needed to make use of these buttons.

It can generate weekly overviews, calculate weekly or yearly totals, and compile listings of all workouts meeting certain criteria. Version Changes 1.14 - allow Comments field to be blown up, and to receive imported text from a textfile 1.13 - cleanup of progress messages 1.12 - minor added features in total calculation, pulse/weight info - small bug fixes in Tally section - layout change on Global Info card - corrected some problems with Help text 1.11 - fixed bug in bug fix for version 1.10 (oops) 1.10 - bug fix in weekly totals calculation

title: LinguaFlash - vocab helper
description: This is an upgrade of Italiflash -- the HyperCard stack I wrote to help me improve my vocabulary for Elementary Italian class. It can be used to help you learn vocabulary of most any foreign language. It is based on the flash card metaphor, and it's very easy to use. Words are chosen randomly from a list. When all words in the list have been displayed, ItaliFlash will notify you that it is resetting the word list. As your vocabulary improves, you'll want to change the word list by deleting words you know very well and adding new words you don't know. Just click on ADD WORDS at any time. A list on the right side of the screen will display all the words currently loaded into the program. To save your word list to an external file, click on SAVE LIST. To recall a previous list, click on LOAD LIST. These features allow you to have multiple lists of vocabulary words. Shareware: $5. (A stand-alone application is also available; see LinguaFlash read-me, enclosed). Checked for Mac & HC viruses with Virex 5.6.

One obvious application is the automation of repetitive multiple file processing by AppleEvent aware applications. Documented scripting and help stacks developed using this XCMD for Kaleidagraph 3.0 may be requested from T. E. Moore (TomEM).

title: ShowDialog XCMD 1.6.7r
description: ShowDialog XCMD 1.6.7r Sunday, June 21, 1998 A revised version of the late Jay Hodgdon's ShowDialog 1.6.7 stack, with updated instructions and registration information. The ResEdit instructions have been rewritten for 2.1.1. THE EXTERNALS THEMSELVES HAVE NOT BEEN CHANGED. Includes several bonus externals. From the stack: 'ShowDialog XCMD is an external command that will allow you to display ANY type of dialog or alert that can contain ANY number of buttons, ANY amount of text, and ANY number of icons or PICT resources. You decide whether the dialog or alert is to have a default (outlined) button. You can have as many fields of static text as you wish and can modify any two of these on the fly (in the XCMD parameter list). You can have any number of editable text fields that the user can type into. Any of these fields can contain a default text string (that you specify in the parameter list). Your dialog can have as many check boxes or radio buttons as you want or can have a popup menu or a scrolling list from which the user can select items. You can also create specialized dialogs for the display of interactive help screens, color icon bars, or Macintosh 'splash screens.' You use ResEdit to create the necessary DLOG/DITL resources that allow ShowDialog to function. ShowDialog XCMD takes seven forms: Type 1, Type 2, Type 3, Type 4, Type 5, Type 6, and Type 7. Type 1 allows for 'regular' buttons, icons, PICTs, and static text only. You can modify the text as you wish in the parameter list. Type 2 can have these and ALSO any number of editable text fields that the user can type into and an unlimited number of check boxes. Any of these fields can also contain default text (provided in the parameters). Type 3 shares the same attributes as Type 2 with the exception that you can have as many radio buttons as you want instead of the check boxes. Type 4 is also similar to Type 2 with the exception that this type contains a popup menu. Types 5 and 6 are used to display 'help' screens consisting of multiple PICTs. Type 7 displays a scrolling field from which the user can select lines in a fashion similar to the Font/DA Mover. You specify the font, pt. size, and default line of the list in the parameter list. This type can also respond to any number of 'regular' buttons. (Editor's note, Type 7 ShowDialogs do not work properly under System 7.)' Edited and originally uploaded by Perry Barton (PBARTON).

title: HyperTinker(TM) 1; RollOff
description: --------------------------------------- RollOff -- Scripting that scrolls text horizontally to allow you to see the contents of a one-line field that are hidden due to a field's small size. --------------------------------------- Free! From... The Western Hemisphere Dog & Pony Company (TM) ------------------------------------- Don't you just hate it when you have a million fields you need to place on a card or background and you have a postage-stamp-size-area to work in? The fields are too small to make effective scrolling fields, so the text ends up to be 6-point Tiny Helvetica. Yeah, I hate it, too. So, I came up with a few tricks to avoid pulling out any more hair than necessary. One of them is RollOff. When Apple's Human Interface Guidelines are followed, Mac applications provide a way to edit the text that is hidden off one end of a field that's too short to show it all: the text automatically scrolls as you drag the mouse pointer past the right side of the field. Unfortunately, HyperCard doesn't provide that ability, so I sighed, sat down, and came up with a cheat. When the various parts of RollOff are installed in the appropriate handlers in your stack, you are permitted to see any text that's hidden off one side of a field. (Note that the dontWrap of the field need not be 'true', though it does look a bit better when it is). --- Version 1 of RollOff uses a global variable to store 'Rolled-Off' characters, so data could be lost if the machine dies while RollOff is working. Rare, but... it could happen. --- Version 2 uses fields to store the characters, as well as the id of the field they belong in; if the power goes out whilst you type, don't worry. When you come back to the card, the data will be restored from the field. This version is safer, but it isn't as fast. RollOff may be used free of charge in any private or commercial product, as long as the proper credit is displayed (noted in the card scripts). I hope this works for you. It may not mesh with what you're doing right now, but keep it around somewhere just in case... Ya never know when it might just work perfectly with some fancy project! Have fun! ...Bob West, The Western Hemisphere Dog & Pony Company (TM)

title: HyperTools Stack 1.0
description: eleteMenu all allow you to modify a menu that was created with makeMenu() or in a script of a stack. ThisStack() returns the name of the current stack. FuncErr() returns a function error (if you made a mistake defining a function). IsPythagorean() and pTriples() Tell you whether a triangle is a right triangle (Pythagorean) and find the missing side. ProcessErr() displays the error , beeps how many times you ask it to, displays the desired buttons, and exits to HyperCard(optional). And finally, aboutStack displays the desired 'answer' and string for the stack you want it too. Hope you find these utilities useful and remember to support shareware. *************************************************** Checked with CP Anti-Virus 3.0 for viruses

There is one hell of a lot of information here. The stack is not flashy like some other stacks I have put here. If you are a student of music, it's probably worth your while to get it. This version is free for personal use. If you're an application developer and you want to use it for anything, either freeware, shareware, or commercialware, you must ask my explicit permission. My answer will most likely be yes, but basically I don't want someone making money off the sweat of my brow and not cutting me in on it. If you wish to show your appreciation, money is a very universally accepted form of gratitude. Any amount. If you're too poor for that, a postcard or an Email would be nice. Believe me, I know what doing $50 gigs sometimes and sometimes not, is like. If I was a rich money grubbing SOB I wouldn't be giving this away, right? Play on into the night dudesx C. R. Dick P.O. Box 1591 Corvallis, OR Fax: 503 754 3114

title: UserLevel5
description: This stack contains an XCMD that you can use to force all stacks to remain at userLevel 5. Using Resedit, copy [XCMD 'set' ID 512] from this stack into a copy of your HyperCard application program (not your home stack). Then use the new copy of HyperCard to open those stacks that try to set userLevel down. The only set commands that will work are those that do not alter the userLevel, and those that set it to 5. There is nothing a stack can do to evade this XCMD, short of renaming or deleting it from your program; any stack that tries to modify your application program without your permission deserves to be called a VIRUS . This XCMD is free and in the public domain. Includes source code :)

description: Ok here it is a stack getting closer to what I wanted over the summer..I have a Mac Plus and I wanted to use 8 bit samples with midi at home..Now this stack, which is compiled from several different stacks, has tempo and pitches. The way to play the sounds is command P or under the menu Sound Controls..So to play midi its command ] and it should play together if you put snd reasources in your stack and sequence a midi file in either track 1 or 2. This stack has some bugs in it so please e-mail me if your having problems or you just want to give me some ideas. There will be an update in a mounth or so. Thanks for downloading I hope it is everything you need.

title: Target Strike 2.7

title: The Cauldron
description: This was a place to test Hypercard back in the day... (cauldron = place to mix stuff)

title: Voie Lactee 1.1
description: A game in which you (as the milkman) must collect bottles of milk before a cat gets to them. Instructions in French. Black & White.

title: Watch Hypercard Messages.sit
description: This stack contains a handler which traps and displays all messages. You can slow down all messages and watch each one be displayed in the message box. And you can also record every message into a field. This is a great feature for debugging those troublesome stacks!

title: WorkSheet--A super msg box
description: The WorkSheet is a window that you can summon from any stack in HyperCard and that performs these operations: Executes scripts or expressions. Executes pre-written or user-written utility scripts Saves all text you type in the msg box. Provides an on-line HyperTalk help reference system Displays any text file you wish to choose. Prints part or all of itself. Preserves its text contents from session to session. Other things that I can't think of right now! If you have any questions about this stack please contact the author at these addresses: Hunt Stoddard P. O. Box 861 Saratoga, Ca 95071 USA AppleLink: HStoddard

title: Checking non-numeric XCMD
description: ----------------- An XFCN that checks whether any item in any line of a container is non-numeric. If a non-numeric item is encountered, its item and line number are returned. The XFCN is very small (about 400 bytes) and very fast. It is freeware.

title: Truncate XFCN 1.1
description: Truncate truncates strings according to given width, font and size by inserting ellipsis.

title: HyperFind Find XCMD Window KEA
description: ----------------- HyperFind is a hybrid XCMD/Stack for HyperCard 2.x that helps speed up and simplify searches. HyperFind does this by providing a floating Find window similar to the Find window in your favorite editor or word processor. HyperFind allows you to change Find methods with a click on a radio button, mark and unmark the current card, restrict searches to marked cards only, and restrict searches to a specific background field by clicking in a list. HyperFind does not interfere with handlers that override HyperTalk's default find. HyperFind is great for all stacks!

title: Portfolio Construction
description: This is the Portfolio Construction stack. A hypercard stack that will teach you a step by step process that I developed for creating presention portfolios for photographs or flat work. Based on Japanese book binding techniques this portfolio design is adaptable, elegant and affordable. A freeware offering by the maker of Address Ace the indispensible electronic address utility in HC form. Of Address Ace: 'Simply put, this Hypercard goodie delivers anything you're likely to find in any of the commercial addressbook managers but at a shareware price...Address Ace is a winner. Do try it.' --Bob Brant in 'The Best of Macintosh Shareware,' 1993 Version 2.6 now available on AOL

title: Polybuttons.sit
description: ----------------- XCMDs for producing what appear to the user irregular shaped buttons. excellent for maps or otherr applications where a rectangular button woun't do the job.

title: Christmas Music

description: U.S. Constitution and Amendments broken into easily studiable form.

title: The Chemistry Toolkit
description: If you are a chemistry educator and have wanted a program that will help you in prototyping problems involving moles, molarities, molalities and pH (strong and weak acids and bases) then The Chemistry Toolkit could be the product you need. You can also use it as a classroom visual aid. This HyperCard stack (The Chemistry Toolkit Ad.) contains an overview of what the Toolkit can do. If you are a student, the Toolkit can be a great help to you. You can use it to check your answers - even devise your own problems! Also included in the program is a set of 'recipes', which are notes on how to solve a variety of problems. This is Version 1.0 of The Chemistry Toolkit. Also available from AOL is the 'Molar Mass Calculator' - which is one of the calculators taken from the Toolkit.

Music Maker Magnifier ------------ Ed. Note - Cool Stack - Check it out!

title: LaunchDoc XCMD 1.1

title: Library Stack 1.1.SIT
description: ----------------- The Library Stack is a disk catalogger. It will read the Volume Name, File Name, File Size, and File Type from your floppy disks and put each file on a card. It also attempts to group files with similar names on the same card. It also has a place for you to write keywords and a description. It has sorting and searching capabilities. Shareware. This is an updated version of Library Stack 1.0. It fixes a bug in the find buttons. If all of the associated files have the same name, they should end up on the same card. If they have different names, then they will end up on different cards and you will have to group them manually. Version 1.0 put each file on a separate card and they all had to be grouped manually. It also ignores the folder names, desktop files, and the information type files on Educorp disks which previously ended up on separate cards. In the future I hope to find a better way to group the files so that whatever files are grouped in a folder will end up on the same card.

title: Energy Metabolism: A HyperCard Interactive Lesson

MathTutor allows your youngster to choose one of the basic four arithmetic operations, set up the numbers and answers, and check for the correct answer... all by screen keyboard and buttons. MathTutor displays a running number of right and wrong answers and the grade earned thus far, during each use of the stack. I can be reached on AOL at GJCANNON.

title: Military Ranks

title: Mississippi HyperTextBook 4

title: Modeless Dialogs
description: ----------------- ABOUT: Modeless Dialogs is a collection of four intelligent XFCN's (external functions) that allow HyperCard developers to create modeless and modal dialogs. The XFCN's included in this stack will allow you to create/display as many modeless and/or modal dialogs as you wish, monitor activities relating to the dialogs, and dispose the dialogs when you are through. Using these XFCN's is like using any HyperTalk command. It's that intuitive! WHAT IS A MODELESS DIALOG? Simply put, a modeless dialog is a dialog that can remain on the screen without forcing the user to respond to it before continuing to work. While a modeless dialog is up, a user can still work with HyperCard as if the dialog was not there; typing, navigating etc...Similar to the tool and pattern palettes in HyperCard. EXAMPLES: Modeless dialogs can be used to create button panels that are relocatable by the user (they can even be placed outside the HyperCard window so large screen monitors can be fully utilized). You can use color pictures (PICT resources) in your dialogs and respond to mouse clicks on it too. The uses are really limited only to your imagination. USE: You are free to use these XFCN's for any personal or commercial stackware. All we ask is that you please make a tax-deductible donation to one of the charities listed in the stack. Another intelligent product from Intelligent Solutions!

title: Multimedia/Hypercard
description: This stack is worth the wait. It has lots of neat sounds, and animation too! Also, it shows many basic concepts for scripting. The stack is in a somewhat unfinished state, but it is still very complete and interesting. There is a table of contents (directory), and a palette for navigation. For example the tidbits section lets you type your name into a field, and get greeted when you click on a button, the time, date, and other interesting fields which move information. Stack also includes customized menus, and an audio/visual intro and online help (there's a '?' button on each card). A must to download! P.S. Send any comments you have about this stack to me (screen name DaveKam)

title: Novice Ham Test 3.3

title: Alert Manager
description: ----------------- Alert Manager is a development utility that implements standard type alert dialogs (OK response, CONTINUE response, & OK/CANCEL response with STOP, CAUTION, and NOTE alert types) in HyperCard stacks using Jay Hodgdon's ShowDialog XCMD. To call any type of alert from your scripts, you need only provide the alert text strings and the call to Alert Manager. The Alert Manager scripts handle everything else. As an added feature, you can control the alert dialog display and system beep in varying ways at three different user-configurable help levels. Any required conditional branching is handled automatically. The Alert Manager is very flexible (18 different alert dialogs possible) and easy to use and script. This demo stack provides complete examples, instructons, a tutorial., and automated installation of scripts and resources. Alert Manager is designed to work with version 1.5 of ShowDialog (included). The Alert Manager 1.1.6 Upgrade Script--included with this file--contains the handlers necessary to make Alert Manager compatible with ShowDialog version 1.6.

title: Pops 1.0
description: Version 1.0 6/13/88 I never liked the PopUp XFNC too much, because I liked more control over fonts and selections in the menu. With the advent of HyperCard 1.2, I was able to finally create the tool I wanted using only scripts. All 4 fields here are variations on the same theme. Each you select by clicking and dragging to the appropriate selection. If you option-click a field, the field changes to a scrolling list that you can edit. When the mouse leaves the field, the field is restored to it's original state. I am most pleased with the affect in 'PopDown Selection' and 'PopDown List'. The problem with 'PopCenter Selection' and 'PopUp Selection' is that the item in the field under the mouse changes when you click down. These scripts make use of the following new commands in HyperCard 1.2: 'select', 'is within', 'top', 'bottom',and 'height'.

With fully-documented and easy-to-use Apple events, users can send and retrieve QuickMail messages with products like FileMaker Pro, FoxPro for Macintosh, Frontier, HyperCard and SuperCard. Multiple recipients and file enclosures are supported. QuickAgent will include example FileMaker Pro templates.

I added extensive comments to all the scripts: a NYMUG member almost hit me over the head with the fact that he couldn't understand the scripts. (The checkbox worked normally, just the reseting at openCard that wasn't quite right.)

title: EZ Bike Gear Calculator
description: An attractive, fun, and very easy to use and understand program, THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR will automatically figure out the 'gear/inches' for any bicycle's front chainwheel/rear cog combinations. It also contains a complete easy to read description and history of just what 'gear/inches' are (and why you should care), for those who aren't familiar with the term. Determining and installing the proper selection of gears on your bike will enable you to cope with the enormous variations you may encounter in your riding, not only in road and wind conditions, but also in your body's ability to produce power. THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR is necessary because, unfortunately, the selection of gears on most 'factory-built' bikes is chosen mostly in terms of the manufacturer's convenience, and also, of course, must necessarily reflect an 'average' of all the thousands of riders who buy that brand and model of bike. However, in real life, a dedicated rider with years of experience is going to require a different set-up from a casual novice, and, of course, a rider in a flat area like Florida, for instance, will require a totally different range of gears from a rider in a mountainous area like, say, Colorado. I'm sure that by now you've 'personalized' your bike, choosing gadgets and accessories that reflect your own personal taste. Well, taking it one step further, and insuring that the selection of gears installed on your bike is the selection that's exactly right for you and your riding style, is where THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR comes in. Suitable for both road and mountain bikes, THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR can process data for up to 3 front chainwheels and 8 rear cogs. Using THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR to figure out the 'gear/inches' of your present set-up will help you easily determine whether any of your present chainwheel/cog combinations are duplicates, whether your high gear is high enough, your low gear low enough, or if any of the combinations fall outside the range of gearing that's suitable for your riding style and conditions, as well as the best shift pattern to use to 'step' through your range of gears. And, racers and tri-athletes can also use THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR to 'fine-tune' the selection of gears they might use for a particular event. A long, hilly race would require a totally different set of gears from, say, a flat time-trial, or short, tight-cornered criterium on city streets. So, give THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR a try. It's a great looking, fun and easy to use program that's well worth the download for experienced and novice cyclists alike. And, since it's 'postcard-ware,' it's 'almost' free!

title: radioButton/Field Creator.SIT
description: ----------------- This is a porgram that creates cards that have inter-connected radioButtons and fields. A click on the button or field will close or open the field depending on whether it was previously selected. All scripting is done for you. No XCMDS, pure hypertalk (But it is very fast). I hope you like it.

title: recursion engine 1.4
description: This edition of recursion engine fixes a few very embarassing bugs which appeared in the original. these include: - would crash if userlevel was not set to 5. - was system seven dependent. - one of the cards was dysfunctional. - misspellings and other small errors. what it does: recursion engine generates recursion fractals - the koch curve, snowflakes, dragons, the maltese cross, etc - in outline form. it does this very quickly and smoothly, and does not depend on XCMD's for execution. it is designed with an eye towards experimentation, and is rather heavily documented in the scripts as well as within the cards. it is a bit fangled, and has lots of bells and whistles, even though i have really TRIED to keep the thing as simple as possible without hurting performance. it comes ready with several cards already built to plot recursion fractals, as well as a pi lesson, logarythm chart, and a title page packed with help. you'll be laughing in the face of infinity in no time.

description: Has Shutdown and Restart XCMDs which allow you to safely shut down (i.e. it makes all of the right system calls in the right order) from within a stack.

I thought her idea was great, but my younger daughter needed something even simpler, with no reading involved. So, I designed some stacks that consist of only two cards. They are easy to use, colorful, and fun.

title: PhoneConv 1.01
description: On October 10th 1995 almost all dutch telephone numbers will change to a new 10 digit telephone number. This HyperCard stack will convert your current telephone numbers to the new format.

title: Chord/Scale/Note Conv.sit
description: ----------------- 'By converting scales to notes to chords and back, You can: Discover what scales Mozart or Bartok liked to use. Learn to solo through tricky chord changes. Come up with the perfect chords for a melody you've written. I designed the Converter to help my music students learn by example how to translate between CHORD symbols (such as those found in fake books, lead sheets, etc.); SCALE types (as taught in traditional jazz theory); and real NOTES (such as those produced by real musical instruments or facsimiles thereof). You can also use it for more general exploration: How many dominant chords/scales are there? Are there any scales that have a major 7th, a perfect 5th, and a flat 13th? Is there even a single note that will work through a whole chorus of 'Giant Steps'? If not, then which note will get me the farthest? Why does tritone substitution sound so damn good? What will other substitutions sound like? And most importantly: What kind of chord is THAT? Etc. etc. etc.'

title: Invoice Creator v 1.0 (Demo)
description: An easy to use but powerful invoicing program for businesses or individuals. *Automatic invoice numbering. *Automatic calculation of invoice totals, including sales tax(if any). *Custom headings of many catagories. *Generate aging reports for all unpaid invoices. *Generate total invoices reports by customer. *Automatic crediting of paid invoices. *Print multiple invoices at one time. *Many help features that are accessible throughout the program. *Several different search options. Full featured version available for $20.00.

title: Pathbased Animations
description: Getting tired of having to write huge scripts to tell hypercard how to animate your buttons? Wish you could make fluid, flowing animations with your icons? Have you ever wanted the computer to write words on the screen, or paint a picture with a single line of code? If any of those are true, or if you're just looking for a fantastic utility, 'Dragger' is for you! Dragger is a simple, but effective utility for creating fields and fields of paths. Simply drag the mouse where you want the path. Dragger will automatically show you where your points are on your path. When you're done, release the mouse, and dragger will do all the calculations and give you two fields full of points and commands. If some of this isn't making sense, don't worry! Included in this stack are several demonstration buttons that will show you exactly how to use these paths to animate your button!. That's right, just point, click, drag, and you're done! Then you can go back into your stack, and have your buttons move quickly or slowly across the path you've dragged for them! Because Dragger has a constant rate of recording, you can control the speed of your animations for your buttons, by simply slowing down or speeding up your mouse! Download this fantastic utility now! ===================== Checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6

The stack has fields for the date, mileage, parts used, repairs done, notes and more. Docs included. It works excellent! Sorry to all Plus, SE/20, & SE/30 users, this stack was created for a 13' monitor or larger :(

title: HyperCard Tool Box.SIT
description: ----------------- MacUser magazine welcomes you to the HyperCard Utilities Toolbox. This collection of buttons, cursors, sounds, custom font, resource lister, and assorted utilities, is designed to help you improve the quality of your own stacks. The stack includes 135 buttons (with 15 animated buttons); 77 cursors (with 12 animated cursors); 30 digitized sounds; a custom font with over a dozen special features; a resource lister, and four other handy utilities for advancing stackers. You can transfer all the special resources here directly to your own stacks, without any complicated tools.

title: Recipe Alley v1.2
description: ing recipes that you don't like from the list of recipes that the program will choose from when making its menu. The program also makes sure that you never have the same meal two days in a row. So that you won't have to bring your computer into your kitchen, the computer can print out all of your recipes. After choosing recipes, Recipe Alley can print out a shopping list of the ingredients of the recipes for the next several days (you choose the number of days). Recipe Alley is shareware. I ask that if you find Recipe Alley useful, pay the $10 shareware fee. Comments, suggestions, and questions are welcome.

title: StackTools Menu 1.3

Highlights include: - stack management - object management - script management, searching and reporting - stacksInUse management - window management - palette management - resource management (optional) - save messages as menu items - menu control of card, bkgnd, stack, and global properties - full set of useful hot keys and expanded arrow key functions StackTools Menu runs unobtrusively in the background. It contains all necessary resources and handlers so that you do not need to add anything to your home stack. Fully documented, with printable help. Version 1.3 adds improved script searching, and fixes a few minor bugs. Requires HC 2.1.

title: Archimedes Discovering Pi
description: A Hypercard stack made for a math project, it interactively demonstrates how Archimedes discovered pi over 2000 years ago. Archimedes used an interesting method to approximate the value of pi. Since it wasn't easy to accurately measure the circumference of a circle, he measured the perimeter of a many sided polygon, which was close to a circle. This stack demonstrates this principle. For more hypercard stacks visit Ian's Iconoclastic Internet Imbroglio http://www.concentric.net/~ninpiggy Staff Notes: Interesting Math. Scripted Paint Tools. Scanned with Disinfectant and MerryXmas Vaccine.

The goal is to try to get kids and adults to begin thinking realistically about hazards and relative risk. A standalone version that does not require HyperCard is also available upon request.

With Software Inventory you can keep an up to date record of the software you have installed in your computer. The stack is menu driven. You can automatically generate 4 different categories of software lists. The software lists can be used to generate inventory cards of your choice. You can save typing effort by automatically transferring vendor information to the inventory cards from a file of over 200 vendors. You can add new vendors to the list. Online help is available for each of the four sections of the program. This is version 1.0b4. It includes changes that also let it run on earlier versions of HyperCard. It has been tested on HyperCard versions 2.0v2, 2.1 and 2.2. A more complete description is contained in the Read Me file that accompanies the stack.

title: Waldo v4.1.1
description: WALDO 4.1.1 (SM) is a HyperCard HyperText program. It requires HC v 2.0. WALDO turns plain text files into HyperText documents. There are many HyperText indexing and multimedia features in WALDO. Waldos may include graphics, sound and QuickTime Movies. Waldo is ShareWare. Waldo creates Digital books, magazines and documents. Waldo 4.1.1 is a bug fix for HC v2.1. users only. XHome (SM) v1.2 is a bug fix. Waldo includes XHome 1.2 & Waldex (SM). Waldo and Soto (SM) allow the creation of very sophisticated HyperMedia Documents. You may embed graphics, sound, QuickTime movies and text in words. You may use Soto to import any text document. Soto may also be used to import text in a variety of structured formats including; SeText, Dart, Waldo, MacWeek, NewsBytes. While Soto may be used as a stand alone text reader for these formats it becomes a way of 'importing' prestructured text into Waldo. Text in Soto can be exported into Waldo format. When your text is in Waldo HyperText links and tables may be easily constructed by clicking on text. These links may have attached graphics, sound, QuickTime, or text. The links or spots are assembled into HyperQueues. These Queues may easily be restructured by clicking in list views. There may be up to 10 Queues in a document. Queues may be erased or saved to disk and later reloaded. New Queues may be created at anytime. Registered users will be given a 'KEY' to unlock advanced 'OpenBook' features including; CD-ROM support, locking selected features out from reader access, further customazation of locked books, creating an integrated book with all media locked inside of book. While Waldos in the 'OpenBook' state are locked the reader can still create HyperQueues that utilize all of the HyperMedia features--Queues that have been locked by the author are protected from reader modification or erasure. XHome is an enviroment for MultiMedia creation and structuring. Graphics and sound created in XHome are saved as 'resources' in the XHome stack. Media, including QuickTime movies, created in other programs can be pasted or integrated into XHome. When ready the user compiles the media into an new XRes (SM) stack combining all media together with an automatically created script. This new XRes stack can be accessed from within the Waldo by simply calling up a list and clicking on a selection. There is too much more to include in this discription. Waldo is ShareWare and the registration fee is a $10.

title: Hackintosh 1.7
description: Hackintosh is a cool program I wrote whilst completely toasted on caffeine. (Come to think of it, most of my software is like that. You don't think 10-12 cups of coffee a day is too much, do you?) I designed Hackintosh for a friend of mine. He had an old laptop (5300c@117MHz) and the Finder really crawled. So, he asked me if I would write a command-line for him so he could work with his files more quickly. I picked Linux as my model, and HyperCard as my platform. For the file access, I chose to use an adaptation of some HyperTalk/AppleScript modules I'd written (which eventually became Hackintosh Core Services). Although the first version used the shell commands directly via 'start using,' subsequent versions have used a Virtual Memory Module, to load an almost unlimited number of shell commands. What started out as a quick hack for navigating and manipulating files quickly turned into something far more powerful. Because of the inherent extendability of the HyperTalk scripting language, plus the OSA and XCMD/XFCN interface, Hackintosh can be programmed to do just about anything, completely via shell commands. You can write shell commands for complex file management, resource control, even control over the internet. In fact, if you can find/write/hack a way to do something in HyperCard, you can probably make Hackintosh do it.

A script storage section is provided. (Yeah, it's been done before and after I wrote this, and maybe done better, but this one's mine.) Converted to HC 2.0 and author address data changed, but not updated to take advantage of the new tools. Click the mouse for more info. title: Cloze Text.sit
description: To aid in correcting the cloze test, only the _exact_ word is considered correct, so if a student answers #23 with 'also' and the original word inthe document is 'and,' it is marked incorrect. To offset this, the grading scale for the test is rather lenient. If most of the students are able to correctly fill in 45%-55% of the blanks, the document is considered to be at the proper reading level for the students. Numbers higher or lower than these percentages may indicate that the document is too easy or too hard for the students. While there is a program similar to this in the AOL archives, that program contains pre-defined text. _This_ program allows you to import a document (such as an essay-test) which has been saved as a TEXT fileand run the cloze text on the document. It creates a new file with the words blanked out (and numbered for easy reference) and a list of the words that were removed (also numbered, for easy grading). This program is handy for teachers that write their own exams and are concerned with the reading level that the test is being written at, or likewise for an assigned book (the instructor would have to type in part of the book's text...). Freeware. If you like it, drop me a line, though!

title: Reservoir Simulator
description: This program simulates reservoir operations on the Columbia River system. Several species of salmon have been declared threatened or endangered in the river system. Use this simulator to find a way to better operate the reservoir for the salmon and for the other uses of the reservoir-- flood control, power, resident (non migratory) fish, and recreation. A fun simulation game that lets you operate a hydro power reservoir and try to save the Northwest salmon! See the impacts on recreation, flooding, power, and fish of your own strategy for operating the reservoir. This is a fun learning tool and will give useful insight into how reservoirs operate, and also the kinds of social tradeoffs that have to be made in operating the reservoirs. The issues raised in this program are real life issues being addressed by reservoir operators in the Pacific Northwest. This program is an upgrade of an earlier version. The primary change is you can now keep track of your reservoir operation through the year, and print out a graph of the operation and the relative benefits. It also fixes a few minor bugs, and has greatly reduced memory requirements (found dormant code).

title: SafeShutDown XCMD.SIT
description: When it became apparent that there were some risks in using the very popular ShutDown XCMD, a few members of the MacNET XDeveloper Exchange scrambled to their development environs to come up with at least one SAFE method of providing direct shut down from a stack. SafeShutDown works in two modes: 'Modal' or 'Quick'. If you simply call the external with no parameters, SafeShutDown displays a Modal Dialog, providing 1 of 5 choices for an action, 'Shut Down', Restart', 'Home','Finder' or 'Cancel'. A check box allows the user to decide whether or not to 'zero' the stack prior to the action. The dialog also provides additional stack-specific info. If you use the 'quickParam', you can have SafeShutDown bypass the dialog completely. Use the following letters for their corresponding actions: 'S' - Shut Down; 'R' - Restart; 'H' - Home; 'F' - Finder.

title: DefaultCurs.SIT
description: DefaultCurs Stack 1.01 by Dean Wette, XNet 27 February 1989 Have you ever wished you could change the default cursor in HyperCard from the hand to something more normal, like an arrow? Me too. In fact I don't like the hand...it looks stupid, especially in a stack where you've spent a lot of time constructing an elegant user interface. If you don't agree then you might as well go to the 'File' menu above and select 'Delete Stack'. Otherwise, if you want to get rid of the 'hand' cursor in your stack permanently without the necessity of an inefficient or impractical idle handler, then read on... The hand cursor in HyperCard actually lives in 2 resources. The first, and most difficult to alter, is the (until now) permanent default cursor for HyperCard. This cursor and it's mask are in the '_12' font in HyperCard. The other 'hand' cursor looks exactly the same; it's the one used when you give the command, 'set cursor to hand,' from a script. It's a real CURS resource. To permanently change the default cursor to something other than the hand, you will have to do three things: edit the CURS resource and edit the '_12' font, and, finally, you have to include the following command in your openStack handler to activate the new default cursor: 'choose browse tool'. This will insure that the real hand is purged for the new default cursor. This editing of the default cursor was done with HyperCard 1.2.2. I can't guarantee it will work with future versions of HyperCard. All that needs to happen is for Bill to change the font ID or the way the cursor works and everything's gone to hell-in-a-handbasket. So keep that in mind.

title: ChangeObjectLayer.SIT
description: While developing a stack containing numerous fields and buttons, I found myself constantly using the Send Farther and Bring Closer commands in the Objects menu in order to move objects (buttons and fields) through different card and background layers. In some cases I wanted to obscure other objects, and in other cases I wanted to prevent objects from being obscured. Well, when you have 20 or more each of background and card buttons and fields, this menu selection (or its command key equivalent) business to change object layers can become quite tedious, boring, and unfriendly. Therefore, I developed 'ChangeObjectLayer' to automate and speed-up the process of moving objects through different layers. 'ChangeObjectLayer' works with both card and background buttons and fields. Try it out! On this card are six each of card and background buttons and fields. Click on any one of these objects to change its order in the object layers.

title: CMU Tunnels
description: Newsgroups: alt.college.tunnels Subject: CMU Tunnels Hypercard stack (long) Date: 14 March 1994 Folks, I found something lying around on one of my old Mac disks that may interest some of you: an interactive hypercard map of the CMU steam tunnels. I wrote this stack back in '88 as part of a student application fair called StraightTalk. It's very simple, basically a map with hot spots that take you to pages describing the entrance or tunnel you clicked on. It was intended to demonstrate how you could do neat things with a Mac in a short period of time. It's not up to date, but I thought I would post it just for fun. Those of you who haven't actually been in tunnels can get a sense of how they are sometimes laid out, and I thought ex-CMU people might get kick out of reminiscing about old times. For current CMU people, you'll note the conspicuous absence of Resnik Hall & West Wing, the Parking Garage, and anything else that happened after 1988. Don't take this stack as an authoritative reference, but as a peek into the mid/late 80's CMU culture. The StraightTalk fair was officially sponsored, and no, I didn't get into any trouble for presenting this stack. I thought I would, and held my breath when a couple of Security folks came over, but nothing happened. Apple even had a photographer take pictures of all the presenters, including some of me near a tunnel entrance! I never saw the pictures, though... :( Anyway, here's the stack (called "Tunnels"). Remove everything above the "this file must be converted with binhex 4.0" line and then run it through binhex. Feel free to pass this on to other folks, put it on servers, ftp sites, etc. If you do, please send me a note letting me know so I can have a feel for its distribution. Ideally, I'd like it if the text portion of this message was included as a readme file so new folks would know some of the history. Enjoy, Dennis

title: Palette Manipulation
description: LIBRARY: MHC:Utilities Palette is a simple XCMD which changes the current color palette on your machine. Why?? Well simple, what if you were doing QuickTime movies (like I was) and your target only had 8 bit video cards. QuickTime does not remap the Color LookUp Table (CLUT) it, instead, dithers it's colors and there is a noticable degradation in color quality on some video, which as a rule is 16 bit color. Palette can extract the best 256 colors and build a color palette which is stored in the resource fork of the stack. It can then change the systems color palette so that when the picture is displayed it looks best it can on an 8 bit monitor. Source is included. Any problems please contact JerSquard.

title: Eng./Spanish HC children's st...
description: Hi folks - This upload includes four original children's stories, presented in both English and Spanish in HyperCard stacks. I originally wrote these stories for my elementary school students. Some of the stories are whimsical and fun. Last month I uploaded the stories to the Macintosh Education Forum library with a suggestion that these stories might be nicely presented in HyperCard or HyperStudio format. Richard Wanderman, a talented HyperCard using educator from Connecticut, did a lovely job of creating these freeware stacks. Sofia Medina, a teacher from Sarasota, Florida, jumped in to assist with some of the translations. Hope these stacks prove useful to other teachers and parents. If there's anyone out there who can assist in some further translation work, I have a few other children's stories I'd like to share as freeware. It's really wonderful when educators are able to collaborate in this way. The fruits of our joint work can be enjoyed by all of our own students, as well as everyone else's students, too. The next challenge is to create some freeware quiz stacks which can quiz students on both the English and Spanish words used in these stories. Any volunteers to assist in creating the quiz stacks? Cheers, - Phil Shapiro Washington DC Internet: pshapiro@aol.com Oh, by the way, also enclosed in this archive are screen shots from these stack, imported into MacWrite II. These can be printed out quite easily from MacWrite II, ClarisWorks, or Microsoft Word. Note: The screen shots print better on laser printers than on inkjet printers. I like having a few copies of the screen shots printed out to show friends and colleagues who are not Mac users.

title: AddTimes XFCN
description: ----------------- AddTimes XFCN will add together a list of time entries. You can use return-delimited or item (comma) delimited lists. The XFCN will correctly calculate negative time entries, so you can provide billing credits if necessary. You can also have the resource cancel the calculation and flag you if any colons are missing in the list. You don't need to include colons normally--the XFCN will accept integers. An added benefit is that the resource will strip text from the line, so you don't have to worry if there is a descriptive statement in front of each entry on the same line. However, if that text includes dashes or colons you may have problems, for obvious reasons. Use is free in non-commercial stacks, provided credit is given in the stack. Copyright 1990 by George Pytlik. All rights reserved.

title: Hypercard/Hypertalk Help!
description: TYPE: Freeware application SYSTEM 7: Compatible REplaces 'Hypercard Help 2.2' in MHC library Author's email address: clas005@csc.canterbury.ac.nz Author's notes: Here is HC/HT Help, a DocMaker stand-alone. This is information giving help with HyperCard and HyperTalk. It replaces my earlier HyperCard Help, but the only difference is that the earlier version was created with a different app, Misebirakesu, which turned out not to work on every machine, whereas DocMaker is more robust and better supported. I use this as my primary reference for HyperCard and HyperTalk information when my memory fails me. Sections on Hidden HyperCard Features, Reserved Words, Messages, Properties, Functions, and more. Often explains things more clearly or more correctly than the official documentation (IMHO). Forum Note: This stack is highly recommended because it covers previously undocumented features, quirks, and tricks. He also corrects the many errors in the official Apple documentation. This is a must-have for any serious HC developer.

title: Animation Stack
description: I don't know David Brown, but I thought I'd give him a hand as his original "Animation.sit" stack had a couple of bugs in it. Like the fact that he didn't include any icons for the "Begin" button (the one with the "Stop" and "Go" icons which I added -- I apologize for the poor artwork) and the fact that the "Continuous" button didn't work due to the deletion of a "Wait 30" command. But all is restored and things now work wonderfully! Please support this stack with your shareware fee to David as it's (now) a pretty good stack. And, Dave, hope you don't mind that I altered the original stack. Of course, you get the credit not to mention additional shareware fees! --William G. Hutchison, M.D. Boston, MA November 22, 1990 To use the stack properly (Dave was a little nebulous on this), first click on the "Do It!" button to see what happens with the HyperTalk script I generated! Then to create your own animation, erase my picture using the eraser tool, copy a smallish picture from a Clip art stack or one of your choosing, and paste it into the rectangle (The location of the rectangle is really not essential, it just makes things neater for this stack. You could place the picture anywhere on the card, since the "Do It!" button calls for the lasso tool and the picture is on the "Card" level--it will get selected no matter where it is). Then click on the "Continuous" or "Point to point" button. Then click the "Go" sign and follow the directions in the field below the picture you pasted. You should see HyperTalk script being generated in the field on the right. The next step is to click on the "Set up" button which transfers the script into the "Do It!" button's script. Then click "Do It!" and watch your animated picture go wild. Oh yeah, you may notice that if you use a large picture such as the one I substituted, and if you specify a position too near the card's edges (the starting point being near the character's nose or near the middle of his head) that the stack draws lines instead of moving your picture around. This can be remedied by not going so near the edges with your clicking! To utilize the animation in stacks of your own, simply make sure the picture is in the same relative position in your stack as the one in this stack. Then copy the "Do It!" button into your stack. Modify the "Do It!" button to your liking so that the name doesn't show and/or create your own icon button using ResEdit (Open ResEdit, open the Animation stack, then create a new resource as "Icon" and not "Icon#", use the "Icon editor" to draw the icon picture to your liking, then copy it and paste it into the Animation stack ResEdit window, save the changes, quit ResEdit, open your own stack, create a new button and then double click on it, displaying the button's dialogue window, click on the "Icon" button, and choose the icon picture you created in ResEdit, then copy the script from the Animation stack's "Do It!" button into your new button and you're all set!! Good luck and have fun with this stack!

title: PaletteXFCN.SIT
description: The Palette XFCN by Oscar F. Hills 1988 Version 1.03b1 This XFCN was actually written after a conversation at the Boston Mac Expo in which a gentleman who shall remain nameless almost convinced me that it was impossible to write a reasonable HyperCard external which would allow the user to install an additional Pull-Down, Tear-Off menu like the 'Tools' menu, but one in which the user could customize the selections and have them do anything he/she wanted. I then decided to undertake the challenge, and have been working on it ever since. The result is here before you, something in between a kludgey workaround and a nice addition to HyperTalk. As in the 'Tools' menu, the little tool icons are actually font characters which you can change into anything you wish using ResEdit or a Font Editor. Then you can write HyperTalk scripts to define what each of the 18 selections will do. The menu can be pulled down, torn off, hidden, and shown exactly like the 'Tools' (or 'Pattern') menu. There are additional features explained later, which the 'Tools' menu does not have.

title: Pi In The Sky
description: Pi In The Sky October 23, 1994 This freeware stack is an update of the earlier stack. It includes a Pi menu and Help screens that are keyed to the active card. The stack is not protected but the user level has been set to painting to prevent accidental damage. You can change the user level by means of the Protect Stack menu item. If you don't see the menu item in the HyperCard File menu then hold the command key while you mouse the File menu open. The stack script also includes several handlers that are used to dynamically modify the script under certain conditions. The stack can be used to begin to explore the development of the concept of pi. The stack gets its title from the book Mathematics For The Million by Lancelot Hogben. The title was created with Aldus Type Twister. Other sources are listed in the stack. The value of pi is approximately 3.14159. Archimedes calculated the value as more than 3 1/7th and less than 3 10/71s. Computers have been used to calculate the value of pi to many decimal places. HyperCard internally uses 3.14159265358979323846 as its value for pi. I would appreciate any and all comments and suggestions. You can contact me by mail at: Arthur V. Dieli, Harbor Road, Henderson Harbor, NY, 13651-0009 or by email at: ArthurD12@AOL.com or DieliAV@eWorld.com.

For more information, e-mail to TSTADER or stop by the Ham Radio Club conferences every Sunday or Wednesday nights at 1-0 PM East Coast time in our Conference Room.

A build Order window lets you access commonly-used scripts from script files. Just name the function or procedure in your script, and include the script file in the Build Order window: Developer Edition Interface will find it automatically and extend your script to include that handler. No special encoding is needed. Symbol Table management has never been easier: now a single modeless window lets you review all tables, and mark, unmark, install and remove symbols instantly. There s even a handy find button for finding symbol table names anywhere in a list. There is much more. Every CompileIt! user should be making use of this interface. The user will also be able to take a timed test. At the end of the test, the date, score, and time it took the user will be displayed and saved. You will be able to view the scores with a password. The password is: FLASH009. Scores can be printed out or cleared. WARNING - the stack automatically deletes scores after 100 names have been entered. FlashAttack! is a shareware program. It provides a place to record bibliographic info (and convienently names the stack after the book in question), provides some user preference settings and a neat search function that is better than HyperCard's standard one. title: Rites of Passage v2.2
description: READ ME! RITES OF PASSAGE A SCIENCE FICTION ROLEPLAYING MODULE ON HYPERCARD Rites of Passage is a series of HyperCard 2 stacks that comprise a systemless science fiction roleplaying module. You have just downloaded the Menu and Introduction/Character stack. The aim of the Rites Project is to release the entire module (including printable character sheets and GM notes) on HyperCard for free distribution and play. THE NATURE OF THE STACKS The two introductory stacks are large, each over 500K when decompressed. They together comprise 85 cards giving a fifteen minute sound, text and graphic introduction to the module followed by selections from the Characters� stack. The introductory stack does NOT contain the full module: it will be released after convention play in early 1993. Rites of Passage is written and scripted by John Hughes of The Wyrm�s Footprint. It is an experiment in electronic module design and distribution. A convention version of the module will run at CanCon (Canberra, Australia) in January 1993. The completed stacks will be available through the Net shortly thereafter. The stack is postcardware, and may be freely distributed, as long as it is not altered in any way. If you have any suggestions for improvement � scientific, programming, dramatic or jest plain old english expression then PLEASE let me know. The introductory stack contains full information on the module and on the further stacks. E-mail me through InterNet at john.hughes@anu.edu.au Rites of Passage is a systemless and thematic module � a module designed to explore a number of themes or moral quandaries using mood, atmosphere and depth of characterisation. Set in the 24th Century, it is designed to explore the cultural and psychological impact of computer implants and virtual reality. While focussing on characterisation and drama, the module also features a murder mystery, a black hole, possible alien contact... oh, just download and explore the stack. HOW TO USE THE STACK Rites of Passage requires HyperCard 2 and System 6.07 or higher. It will run on any Mac with the necessary memory. As it uses a large number of digitised sounds, a minimum of 4 Meg RAM is recommended. The Introduction (and subsequent stacks) use resources stored in the Menu stack, so they should not be separated. VERSION 2.1 Versions 1.x of these stacks used MacIntalk, a voice generating utility no longer supported by Apple. The MacIntalk scripts caused system crashes in certain Mac II machines, and they have been deleted in the current version. In addition, several cards have been added or clarified in the current version. The original large Introduction has been split into two stacks. Oh yeah, and the spelling... I'd be grateful for comments or reviews. REALLY. Please mail me at the address above. Free Information! Free Imagination! John Hughes
